entries.sign: Hold the loosely flexed open right hand in front of the right side of the body. Carry it upward, outward and down, drawing it in towards the right side.

entries.note: With a long time it means s#A LONG TIME AGO#e.
entries.sign: Point the right index backward over the right shoulder.

Hold both S hands, backs up, near left breast, briskly swing both down to left side, opening them with a snap and giving a slight rebound to the hands after the movement, as though emphatically throwing away something. Sometimes only one hand is used. Compare Bad, Hate, and Charge. See Divorce.
Left hand out flat, palm up, right S hand on it, thumb up. Compare Sit down.
Hold the flat left hand pointing forward, up and to the right, encircle it several times with the right G finger. If possible, make it concrete by indicating the very thing that was encircled.

headword.sign: Bring the flat left hand, back up, in front of and a little to the left of body; left forearm horizontal, fingers pointing to right and front; bring the flat right hand, back up over the left in a semi-circle upward large or small, as best suggests the actual distance. Has been used for More than. Compare Beyond.

entries.sign: make the sign lower opposite the left breast and inward toward one's self.
Thrust G finger many times in different directions over and parallel to the part. Compare Wound and Sick.

Hold the flat left hand out, palm down, and pointing forward and toward the right, pass the flat right hand edgewise across the back of the left. If but one person is meant, the right G is sometimes used. This sign as illustrated is often used for Council. See Council and Laws.

headword.sign: See Work.

Flat left hand pointing to the right and front, palm up, forearm horizontal; the palm of flat right hand is placed on top of left hand a number of times, the left hand being raised a couple of inches each time, to meet it; the movement ends with left hand as high as top of the head. Often it is done as in the illustration but with palm of left down.
Kissing the back of the hand. (Pop.) Not Indian, but they understand it now. (R. B.)
Both flat hands back up, pointing forward, tandem, right in advance, six inches ahead (the fingers extended) moved forward together in gentle jerks. Compare Move camp.
Flat left hand back up, pointing forward, breast high; place G right just before it, then turn right G into V to mean Looking. Also used for Scout. Compare Ahead.
Sign Talk, Make, Way. (C) Sign Help and Talk would be near it.

Sign Heart then shake it up and down two or three times, to indicate the throbbing action of the heart under influence of fear. Or more strongly, sign Heart and then finish by raising the hand until its back strikes the chin, to mean the heart rises in the throat.

headword.note: Seger says this means Buffalo backing out of fight; that is, “drawing in his horns.”.

note.sign: Point right G in several directions, then add Afraid, Not.

G fingers pointing forward at an angle in front of body; left in advance. Draw the right over and behind the left. Draw it back a little way for a little bit after; but far back and low down for a long way behind. Some finish by clenching the right hand.
Make a circle of right thumb and index and sweep it over the afternoon half of the sky from the zenith down. Compare Sunset.
See Repeat or More.
Thrust the tips of the flat right, back out, square against the palm of the flat left held pointing level forward, back to left. See also Oppose. Compare Quandary.
Sign Whiteman and Chief, Give all. The Southern Cheyennes sign Chief and pull teeth, because their first agent had false upper teeth.
See Excite.
Sign You, I, think, same. Sometimes use Equal. See also Treaty.
See Treaty.
Sign Time and point back over the right shoulder with right finger G. Sometimes the thumb or the whole hand is used instead of the index. See Back.
Hold out the left G pointing forward and up; swing the right G over the left to a place in front of it, both pointing the same way. Some finish by closing and lowering the right fist. Compare After.
Hold out flat left, back up, near breast, pointing forward and slightly upward; then hold right G just before it. Compare Advance Guard, which it exactly resembles, except that this omits Looking.
See Wind.
Indicate from what, then drop right V fingers downward onto flat left palm. See Dismount.
Make the signs Face and Equal.

Hold index of right hand upright, move it about shoulder high, forward in long slow zigzags sidewise, always turning it so as to move palm forward. Also used for Be or Exist. See Life, Deer, and Nothing. The Blackfeet use the sign Grow for this idea. See Wandering.
With right hand flat and back up, describe a large horizontal circle, shoulder high.
Both 5 hands in front of body, backs out, right nearer; loosely brush fingers of right on left palm, moving right outward, then reverse and repeat. Sometimes begin with sign All. See Wipe out.
Form two circles with thumbs and index fingers, and link them together, other fingers closed. Some use only index fingers hooked together.
See Good or sometimes Alive.
Hold up the left G, pointing upward, forward, and to the right; strike on it with right G near the tip, then every inch or so up to the arm. See Many times, Cheyenne and Buy.
Move the right G hand, pointed upward, slowly forward and to left in a line slightly waving to right and left. Compare Alive, Life, Man, One and Up there.
See Now.

With elbow at side, hold the right G hand pointing forward; move hand forward, describing circles with the index, the result a spiral, ending with the index raised. (Frosted, borrowed from the Deaf Code.)
Sign Long time, Wiped out, and Not. Sometimes sign Stop, Not. (Blackfoot signs.) See All the time.
Indicate a person, then sign Push and Rising man, or omit last.
See Nationalities.
Lay the flat hands, palm in, on belt, then add Shoot by shooting the right G forward. (Not Cheyenne, but understood.)
Hold the left 5 hand in front of neck, pointing upward, move right G index (pointing down) in and through. Sometimes use With.

Repeat the sign for Father several times, with the flat left hand held back out on the breast, and each time pushed farther away, the Father sign made beyond it. (Crow sign.) The Cheyennes sign Father and Old.

The spread flat right hand, breast high, back forward, drawn six inches to the right and closed to flat hand. (Deaf sign.) Compare Horse. The Cheyennes use Equal or Increase, according to the sense; or sometimes With or Add.
Twist the A hand against or near the forehead.

The compressed right hand, back up, advanced in short jumps, as in Frog and Weasel. Sometimes the sign Ground is made, first by sweeping the flat right across, palm up. “Leaping” is generic for the quadruped as “Flying” is generic for Bird. Compare Jump. Used by Blackfeet. The Cheyennes considered it incomplete.
See Exterminate or Wipe out.
See Trouble.
See Rub it out.
Hold out flat right, back up, swing it slowly up, out, far to right and down low, turning it palm up. Compare Fall and Lie down.

As above, but use right G. (Blackfoot.) In this, as usual, the index up alone means Man. This gesture is so natural that many whites use it; as, for example, in saying impatiently: “That was another man altogether.”
Push right G from the mouth in the sign Talk, then draw back reversed; that is, pointing to one's own face or ear. (Blackfoot.) The Cheyennes use Talk, Arrived here.

note.note: The word that follows the other.
note.sign: Right G index upright on lips, left six inches ahead and parallel; move them together toward the person. (Frosted, borrowed from the Deaf Code.)
Bring the L hands palm toward and alongside of the head, near the base of the ears.
See Want.
Place the right A hand near left side, elbow high; draw it down and out to right side in a shaky curve. (Deaf sign.) Use Here and There. (Cheyennes.)
See Indian.
See Come into view.

Hold up flat right hand, thumb toward self, shoulder high; throw it forward and turn palm toward self, fixing the eyes on it and sign Same. Sometimes use the sign for Look before Same. (Frosted; probably adopted from Deaf Code.)
See Approval.

Hold partly bent left hand well in front, breast high, to left side, palm to you and right similarly to right, but quite near to you; move the latter slowly forward toward former, but not to touch it by several inches. Some use right G. See Arrive there and Quandary.
Make the motion of clapping the hands, but without noise. A white man's sign, but now generally understood.
Hold out right G, back down; raise the arm with a swing and snap and bend the wrist till the finger points straight up. For a large number, use both 5 hands.
See Numeral.
See About.
With flat right, slightly curved, back out, strike half a dozen times in a circle, turning to watch the hand; then add Good. Sometimes omit Good. Or, sign Work and Fix. See Ready.

note.note: To seize hold of and tie at wrists.
note.sign: Sign Seize and then add Prisoner; that is, cross the wrists, hands closed. Sometimes the upright left forearm with S hand, back to left, is held near the left shoulder, grasp left wrist with right hand and pull it a little distance to right.

Hold the flat left hand, back out, near the breast, fingers pointing to right; carry right G, back to front, well out in front of body; bring the right hand briskly against back of left. Often the flat right is used instead of right G.

Hold the flat left hand, back to front, well out in front of body, about height of neck, pointing to right; bring right G hand, palm outward, in front of and close to neck, carry the right hand out sharply to strike the palm of the left.
A haughty lifting of the eyebrows and sidelong, disdainful look down as upon an inferior. (Scott.) Sign Head, Big. In the popular code, indicate big chest. See Conceit and Pride.
Make, with a long swing, the motion of drawing an arrow from the left hand.

Both hands, G fingers parallel, level, forward near right side; carry them over to left in similar position. (A sign borrowed from the Deaf, Frosted.) This is the same as Who; only the context can show which is meant. Sign Same or Beside.
Indicate the object (hill, tree, etc.), then press right G against it, raising the same in jerks. Compare Famous.

Flat hands pointing up, palms in, close to face and moved in till the wrists crossed, right nearest the face; bow the head a little. Compare Blind and Dark. The bowing of the head was not generally done, yet is the only feature to distinguish it from Dark.
Cover the face and eyes with both hands. (Pop.)
See Shame.
Sign Fire and Powder.
See Beg.
See Indian.
Lay the flat palm of left hand over the open mouth and draw the body backward. Sometimes, also, raise right hand flat, palm forward.
See Lost.
See Horseman.
Hold left flat hand, back up, pointing partly up; strike the back with right flat hand.
See Try.
See Call.
Sign Father (or Mother) and Sister. Or, sign Woman with right, then tuck compressed right, point down, under left arm pit. (R. B.)

Both hands, backs down, half closed, thumb and finger tips together, raised very high and spread with a sweep to indicate flashes. It should be done facing north. It is helped if the hands when at the highest are swung apart in an arch.
See Motor car.
Make the sign for Tree with both hands, then for Leaf with the right near the left finger tips, then drop the leaf with tremulous, wavy motion down and to right.
Hold up G hands, move them toward each other as in Meet, but carry left well outside, past and beyond without meeting. Compare Meet, Trade, and Mistake.
Bore right G finger into left palm. Or, over the left G as in sewing.

Hold out the flat right hand, back to right, wrist bent downward. Make as though chopping with it; that is, strike down once or twice. Some also grasp it near the elbow with left index and thumb, but the Cheyennes omit this. For Hatchet, indicate Size. See Tomahawk.
Swing the flat right hand (sometimes S hand) in the hollow of the left arm as though it were a baby. Add signs for sex and size when needed. Compare Tomahawk.
Sign Man, Marriage, No. (C)
See Repeat.
Throw right 5 hand thumb first back over right shoulder once or twice. See Ago.
Lay the right V hand on the mouth, as in Lie, then lay it on the back of the right shoulder. (Blackfoot.)
Sign Scold, Talk, and Hide.

Hold out the flat left hand, thumb edge up; with thumb and finger tips of right back down, rub little finger of left. Hadley gives this with the right hand over. It makes a better sign, but I never saw it used that way. Compare Meat and Thin and Oil.
Hold clenched fist, back up, near breast; throw it forward, down, and aside, opening the hand. Sometimes for emphasis both hands are used. Compare Abandon, Charge, and Hate.
Sign Hole, Enter, and Walk. The Blackfeet sign is Striped-face with size and pawing indicated.
See Taste Bad.
Left C hand, back out; drop compressed right into this; then sometimes indicate thickness with flat hands pointing straight up.
Lay the flat right hand on the forehead, draw it up and back to the top of the head. Touch the hair and sign Wiped-out. (Blackfoot.)

Hold the compressed left hand pointing up; encircle it with the right forefinger and thumb. (Chasing Bear.) Not a true Indian sign and not used, but would understand it. (Seger.) Sometimes use Bunch or Few. See Tribe or Troop.
See Done.
Sign Crazy, Drink, House.
Sign Talk, but use index and middle finger against thumb.
Sign White, Soldier, House.
Sign Born and All gone.
See Fins.
These, of course, are not Indian; they differ locally, but the three following are used by most umpires:
See Ashamed.

Hold the L hands low in front, backs up, forming an incomplete horizontal circle, not touching, the index fingers nearer each other than thumbs; swing the hands apart by wrist action so the index fingers point nearly forward.
Sign Kettle, then interlock fingers as in House of logs, to show structure. The Cheyennes understand this, though usually they sign Kettle and Sew.
Lock the fingers of the hollowed hands, backs down, join the thumbs as for a handle, then with the right hand grasp left thumb and raise the hand a few inches. (Sioux.) Compare Corral.
Sign Night and zigzag flight; i.e., flat hands side by side, breast high, flapped first to right side next to left. (C)
Sign Fight, after which make Shoot with each hand toward the other. (C) Compare Fight and Kill.
Open the mouth as in saying “O” and pat it with flattened fingers of right hand. (C) The Cheyennes use Yell.
Sign Water, then bring right L hand well out in front of body, forming a horizontal half-circle. (C)
Sign Gun, then lay left G index alongside right G, the latter one-third ahead. If there is doubt, indicate drawing it on the barrel tip.
Sign Alive and then finish with Now or Past to indicate tense, and Many to indicate plural. Sometimes use Dwell or Recover for this idea. Compare Alone.
Hold out the flat right, slightly hollow; drop it a little with a sidewise quivering to suggest the shimmering of a handful of beads. (C)
Simulate holding beads between the left index and thumb, while threading them with a needle in the right. For Beadwork add a design or sign for Work.

note.note: One picked out of a handful.
note.sign: Right hand flat, palm up, index and thumb joined with the tip of index projecting. (Chasing Bear. Understood by Cheyennes.)<!--TN: Aside may belong with next entry, Bear.-->
Hold out the Y hands, backs up, and strike both down; push both forward in a series of jerks, or swing down, forward and up.
Hold up flat fists near ears, palms forward, to indicate round ears. (Blackfoot.) Some indicate the paws by holding up both curved 5 hands.
As above, but indicate the gray color.
Hang the compressed right hand, point down, under chin. The hand or hands are differently placed for different cuts of whiskers.
Use Kill.
Hold up flat right hand, and look on the palm as in a mirror, then make the sign Good.
Draw the flat hand down near the face, back forward, and sign Good.

note.note: Tail of beaver striking mud or water.
note.sign: Hold left flat hand in front of body, left arm horizontal; strike up against the left palm once or twice with back of right flat hand.
Sign Consider, then Behold. Understood by Cheyennes and Blackfeet, though not well established.
Sign Grow and Same or Arrive there.
Hold flat hands palms up, points forward, one behind the other, left ahead, push it forward, at same time draw back right, then add Sleep.
Sign Fly, Arrow, and One. (C) In Cheyenne, sign Small, Bird, Make, Taste.
See Ahead.
Sign for Time, but hold left hand near breast and swing right forward, up and over. Or sign After, Many Sleeps.
Hold out the flat right hand, palm up, as a beggar does. Swing it forward and upward, then draw it toward self, slightly curving the fingers.
Lay the flat hands together, palms touching, fingers pointing up (or clasp them) and hold them toward the person. A white sign now understood by the Indians. Compare Pray.
With elbow at sides and arms level, push fists forward two or three inches, right a little behind. Or use Go. See Strong.
See Way.
See After.
Hold out flat right, palm up, pointing forward and moved slowly down to below level. Sometimes use both hands. Compare Show.
Sign Think, Straight.
See Possession.
Is the reverse of Above; which see.
With both hands, make as though putting on a belt.
See Below.
Take left index in right finger and thumb and bend the middle joint of it at right angles. Or sign Break very slowly.

With right middle finger and thumb hold tip of right index, letting it project a little; add Bushes. Or, sign Tree, Pick, and Eat. This is a descriptive phrase rather than an established sign, but it is a good illustration of impromptu constructions which are continually made and are at once understood because in harmony with the main principles of Sign Talk. Compare Fruit, Cherry, and Bullet.
Like With, but right G about three inches off left palm. Sometimes use Close.

note.note: Placing on each of two piles.
note.sign: Indicate the event, as Race, then sign Place; that is, hold out partly compressed hands backs up; swing both forward up and down nearly together at finish.
Hold up the flat hands, palm to palm, six inches apart; then thrust the right G on line close past left palm.
Hold left V hand, fingers level, pointing to right (or straight up) and drop right G down between.
See Warning.

Hold the flat left hand, back up, in front of body about ten inches, fingers pointing to right; bring flat right hand, back up, between left and body at same height, fingers pointing to left; swing the right hand upward, outward, and then downward on curve, beyond left hand, turning right hand back down in movement. Compare Fall and Other.
Sign Book and Medicine.

Hold the curved 5 hands with palms toward each other, well out in front of the body, hands a little lower than shoulders and a few inches apart, pointing forward; separate hands, carrying right to right, left to left, keeping them opposite each other. Also used for Long. Compare Great and Long.
See Sheep.
With flat hands at the shoulders, palms down, imitate the motion of wings. Using different speeds for different birds. Compare Fly, which progresses.
See Born.
See Buffalo.
Place the L hand palm down on the mouth.

Bring the right C hand, back outward and upward, a little in front of the body; snap sharply together the tips of the first and second fingers and the tip of thumb against the back of the left flat hand, repeating the motion. Some omit left hand. The Blackfeet make this from the mouth.
Touch tongue with tip of right G and add Bad. Compare Salt, Sugar, Taste, Taste bad.
See Color.
See Indian.
Hold up right V, pointing nearly level forward, opposite right shoulder; left ditto at left shoulder; swing them alternately at each other.

Bring the A hands palms toward each other, opposite and above each shoulder near the neck; move the right hand to left and left to right till the wrists are crossed, right hand nearest body. Compare Fond.

note.note: Drawing from above and spreading out.
note.sign: Hold the flat hands high up in front, palms forward, apart, at arm's length. Lower them a little and slightly push toward the person meant. (C)
Hold both 5 hands over the food, then add Talk upward.

Bring both flat hands, backs outward, in front of and close to eyes, right hand nearest and both hands parallel to face; move right hand slightly to left, left to right; then place the tips of the fingers against closed eyes. (C)

note.note: A wounded buffalo bleeds at the nostrils.
note.sign: Raise the right V hand so the tips of the fingers are pressed one against each nostril; move the hand to the right and downward, giving it a tremulous motion. Add Red. Some omit Red.
See Flower.
See Hill.

Bring the hands together hollowed, fingers straight, little fingers joining, the thumbs somewhat apart, to represent the body of a boat, held before the breast. Push it forward to indicate movement. Add the motion of paddling for Canoe, or Rowing for bigger boat. Usually the Boat sign is omitted; Paddling or Rowing being enough by itself. Compare Bowl.
See Cook.
Hold up the left hand, palm down, wrist a little bent; with right G tap the wrist bone on outer side of left; then add Hard.

headword.sign: Open and close the flat hands like cover of a book, then indicate the lines of writing. Sometimes show the thickness to distinguish it from Letter. Compare Open and Shut.
Flat right in front of and near the body, pointing downward and to front, moved downward and outward on a curve. Compare Dive.
See Lend.
Use Chief.

Sign Or, that is, hold up the left V, pointing forward, and tap each tip of V, and in turn, with right G; then over left V add All with right. Sometimes point to each and add Two. Sometimes use All. Blackfeet use Two and Same.
The left A hand held still, a little advanced, the right A hand touches it and makes the motion of drawing the cord of the bow.
With curved hands side by side, fingers bent, palms up, indicate shape. (C) Compare Boat. For a larger vessel, use Basin.
Hold out both flat hands side by side, backs up; then swing apart and down at right angles, turning the hands at the angle so the backs are out.
Sign Man, Young.
See Bravado.
Touch forehead with N hand.
All fingers of right closed but thumb and index, these form a “C,” which lay on the palm of flat left, pointing forward, thumb up. Sometimes lay it on left shoulder outside. Compare Name.
Sign Fire, Talk, True, and No. (C)
Sign Heart and Strong.
Gently clap the slightly hollow right hand over slightly hollow left hand, then reverse so left is on right and clap them together again; repeat.
Make the motion of seizing a stick, hold it horizontally with both hands and breaking it in the middle. The thumbs finish wide apart.
Sign Sunrise and Eat.
See Wind.
Hold the hand behind the back, hollowed, open and palm up. (Pop.)
Sign Water; hold out the flat hands horizontally in front of body, pointing forward, palms up; and Across.
Like Bit; but raise the hand till near the eyes.

Move the right G hand briskly well in front or to right or left of body; draw the hand with a sweep in toward the body, at the same time curving index finger. Compare Come in which the index is held vertically, and Steal.
Same as Big, but keep the hands flat and palms up.
is the same as above but with palms down. Compare Prairie.
See Done (No. 2.)
See Decrepit.
See Creek.
Lay nearly horizontal N of right hand on lips; draw it away and down, then add Male.
Left forearm across breast; with lower edge of flat right, strike down past left elbow. Probably means relative on the side. (R. B.)
Sign Bowl, then indicate the handle.

Hold the curved G fingers palms toward and close to sides of head; raise the hands slightly and carry them a little to the front. To distinguish domestic cattle, add Spotted. The Navahos reverse this; that is, with them the curved horns as above means Cattle, to which they add Beard, to mean Buffalo.
Sign Fire off; then grasp the forefinger of the hand with the second finger and thumb, so that the tip of it will so extend beyond them and represent the ball.
Hold out the compressed right hand opposite throat, fingers pointing down. Compare Beard.
Hold out curved right 5 hand, back up and forward. See Herd. Sometimes use Enclosure.
Sign Fire and Wipe out.
Like Grass, but breast high; and draw right hand to you and left far ahead.
Sign Push and Work.
Sign All, with right swung to left; then sign One, with left at left side, and pull it to right side between forefinger and thumb of right.
Sign All Go, One, Sits.
Point right G down, forward and to right; hold it a second, then jerk it back four or five inches. (Hadley.)
See Hill.
Sign Money and Trade, making it clear who gives the money.
Tap three times on side of left G index with side of right G index. Compare, All the time, Peas, and While. See Sell.
See Beside.
Hold the pinched index and thumb of each hand as in Time, but half an inch apart. Or sign Time afterward. Compare Sometime.
See Hide.
Strike the palm of the open left hand with the tips of right fingers, then swing right G a little toward the person.

Lay the back of the crooked right G on lips, pointing to front and left, its tip pressed against the thumb, which is nearly straight; then move the hand upward and forward in a curve, straightening out the index finally with a snap, pointing toward the person or thing. Compare Talk in which the action is repeated. See also Name.
Sign Picture and See. Compare Photograph.
Sign Teepee, holding the hands face high; drop the hands together for a foot with energy.

Hold left hand flat, palm down, fingers a little spread; then lay right hand fingers similarly held across at right angles, to indicate the wood laid ready, and add the sign for Fire. (Sheeaka.) Not a true Indian sign, but sufficiently descriptive for the Cheyennes to understand it.
Sign Camp-fire, then raise the right index in a spiral for Smoke.
Give the signs Question, you, camp-fire, and man or briefly make the Camp-fire sign and look inquiringly.
Hold both S hands in front, elbows at sides, thumbs up; drop the hands for six inches with a jerk. (Sheeaka and the Blackfeet.) Sometimes use only one hand. The Cheyennes sign Own, Strong, Medicine.

headword.note: True, clear as day and good.
headword.sign: Sign True, Day, and Good. (C)
Hold up left G finger and with right hand sign Fire on its tip, then indicate length on left arm.
Sign Sugar; then on left G index held upright, show stripes. (C)
Sign Gun and Big.

headword.note: The arrow that failed to stick.
headword.sign: Hold the flat left hand out in front, thumb edge up; strike the palm of it with the forefinger of the right G hand, which then at once rebounds and is thrown forward and down to rest on its back.
Sign Boat and Paddle.
Push forward compressed right, back down, to represent the curved prow, then add Paddle.

Hold up the fists, palms toward each other, about six inches apart, face high; then indicate Go between; that is, hold the left unchanged but thrust forward the flat right hand, palm to left. (Blackfoot.) Compare Between.
Hold imaginary cards in left and deal with right hand.
Sign Deer, High, and lay the flat right hand on forehead so the fingers slightly spread point forward, showing the brow shovel. (Blackfoot.) Sometimes omit High.
Sign Wagon, then raise the hands, palms down, flat, but bent at an angle, up above the head, and move forward about two feet to represent the carriage top.
Sign Wagon, Small, and sometimes add Black.
Both closed hands held opposite the temple as if holding the tump line, the shoulders slightly forward as though bearing a pack.
With one hand make as though carrying a basket by the handle.
See Railroad train.
Hold right G hand, back up, in front of body, index horizontal and pointing to front, thumb pressed against side of index, with the thumb tip just back of second joint; add Shoot. See Ammunition.
Lay A hand on nose, rotating a little out and up. (C) Sign Nose, Short, Dog.
Same as Get but action quicker. See Get.
Indicate gown by sweeping the 5 hands down over the sides and outward; then add Black. Sometimes make the sign of the Cross.
Sign Buffalo and Spotted. (Blackfoot.) Compare Buffalo. Or, sign Buffalo and Whiteman.
See Warning.
Soldier and Ride.

With thumbs and index fingers of L hands make a horizontal circle; then, keeping the left unchanged, indicate centre with right G finger. Sometimes draw a horizontal circle with right G, then drop same down into its centre.
Sign I, Know, Good. Or use True.
Spring the middle finger with a vigorous snap toward the person, the other fingers closed; hand held face high, back up. A European sign given by Butler. Compare There and Defiance.
See Luck.
Lay right C hand on heart, draw it out, that is, forward, a little and with both A hands outline a human figure. (D) Sign Heart, Good, Bad. (Blackfoot.)
Swing both fists from right shoulder forward and a little down in an up curve, away, rising a little, at the same time springing them open.
Similar but reversed, springing the hands open toward one's face.
See Follow.
Sign, Tree, Pick, and Pound. (Blackfoot.) See Berry.
See Indian.
Sign Bird, then Red and with 5 hand on crown show Comb. (C) Or sign Bird and Whiteman.

headword.note: People with one man rising above them.
headword.sign: Hold up left 5 hand, palm to right, pass index of right G hand at several inches above left. (Scott.) The Cheyennes omit left hand; they shoot the right G up over and much down in a long sweep, finishing lower than it began.
Compressed right hand, points up, swung well out in front and dropped a foot to the height of the child. Compare Young.

Hold out both hands, palms up, very low, fingers pointing up and scarcely spread; alternately swing them up and down for six inches. Compare Grass, in which they are held low and are spread widely; also Bushes.

Hold right G shoulder high, back up, a foot in front of the breast, swing it in a circle with a succession of little bounds or up curves, as though pointing at many different objects in succession, the head turned to follow always; then finish by throwing the G finger forward in a curve; or, in some cases, finish by picking up the imaginary object selected, using index and thumb for this. Compare Find, Hunting, and Look.

Use the flat right hand, little finger down, as an axe, chopping first from right, then from left, once on each side. Sometimes do this on back of left hand, which stands for the log. Compare Free, which is two or three cuts on right side only.

headword.note: The day of the shining tree.
headword.sign: Sign Tree, then hold hand with fingers spread and crooked, palm down, level of face. Lower it six inches in short, quick zigzags to suggest glittering or shimmering. (W. C. Roe.) In Snow the hand is lowered in long zigzags for about two feet. See Shimmer. Or sign Middle, Winter, Tree, and Hanging; for the last, hold the curved 5 hands, backs up, at level of the eyes; jerk them a little apart. (R. B.)
Cross clasp the fingers so the tips are within, then raise both index fingers to form the steeple. (Pop.)
Sign Medicine, Talk, House.
Sign Tobacco; then lay G fingers side by side pointing opposite ways; roll one about the other. Sometimes omit Tobacco; sometimes give Cigar (2nd sign) and Little.
Make sign for Town, then add sign for Big. Or omit Big but swing the hands far apart.
Many are indicated by their initial letter enhanced with twisting motion. (D) For some we may use their nickname but this is merely a suggestion.
Sign for City and Centre.
Sign for City and Wind. The Cheyennes call it Big Lake City.
Sign Buffalo Head and point up high to the wall. (Cheyenne.)
Sign City, Chief, and Red Coats.
Sign for City and trousers cut off below the knee; i.e., draw flat of hand down over thigh then below knee and stop, turning edge of hand in. Or sign Big Island City. (Blackfoot.)
Sign City, Chief, and Capotes.

Sign City, then make sign for broad hat with rim curled up by drawing both index fingers across mid-brow, level in front, then twisting them up at the place of the rim. Or “no name, just Big City, nothing distinctive.” (Blackfoot.)
Sign for City and Smoky.
Sign City and Forever.
Sign Father, Chief, Sits. (Cheyenne.)

headword.note: Great Spirit see no blood on these hands.
headword.sign: Lift hands over shoulder, palms up higher than head and add Blood, No. (C) Or, sign Work, Bad, No.
Sign Clouds, then swing the hands wide apart, finishing with palms up at arm's length, up high. Or, sign Clouds, Wiped out. (Blackfoot.)
See Cunning.
Bring the flat curved right hand, back to right, well out in front of body, about height of shoulder; draw the hand in toward the body and slightly downward. Compare Far. See also Soon.
See Coat.
Rotate the flat hands over each other from in front of the face, to over the head.
Look upward, swing the flat hands at arm's length, palms down over the head; then add Rain.
Sign Hard, Fire, and Good.
Hold the L hands near the breast, palms in; swing them down to the waist.
A few inches over the flat left hand, back down, move the right A as though turning the crank of a coffee mill. Or, sign Black Drink. Compare Tobacco.
Close hollow right over hollowed left and shake as tho jingling coin. (Sheeaka; not Indian, but now understood.) See Dollar.
Bring the fists in front of and close to body, height of shoulder, elbows at sides, shoulders drawn in, and shiver. See Winter. Compare Blanket.
With the finger tips of right hand (thumb crooked under) rub circularly on the palm of left hand as though rubbing color. Often add Same or Equal, to make more clear.
Sign Color and touch the hair or eyebrow.
Sign Sun with left hand and then draw the right G finger around it to mean color of sky around the sun. (Sheeaka). Or, sign Color and Sky.
Sign Color and Deer.
Sign Color, Little, and White.
Sign Color and Grass.
Sign Color and lightly brush the right finger tips over the cheek, points to right.
Sign Color and rub thumb nail of left A hand with tip of right G finger; i.e., nail color, white in Indians.
Sign Color and point to any yellow object, such as a straw or dead grass. Or sign Color, Grass, and Dead.
With all fingers of right 5 hand hooked, comb the right side of the head and down as far as the breast two or three times. Compare Woman.
See Battle.

Carry right G hand, back out, fingers up, in a graceful sweep from arm's length to within a foot of one's face. Many use the flat hand swung down and to you, palm under and toward you. Railroad men use the whole arm, swinging it across the body at an angle of 45 degrees, so as to be seen in a dim light.
Hold flat left, back forward, near breast; swing right ditto pointed up from arm's length in against back of left. See Arrive here.
Hold out left hand flat, back out, at arm's length and pass flat right, thumb up, between left and body.
See Easy.
Right hand held forward and up, fingers closed except index, with which beckon by crooking and straightening, the hand not moved. (Pop.) White sign, now fully adopted by the Cheyennes.
Hold out flat left hand, back forward, thrust right G index up, farther off, under and behind, until it appears above.
Draw flat right hand, palm in, down breast, then off and up in curve forward, palm up. (Sheeaka.) Compare Confess. Sometimes use Glad. (Blackfoot.)
Alternately rub left palm over back of right hand, then right over left back, always palm up; then swing both forward. (Sheeaka. Probably from Deaf.)
Hold out the flat right, palm to you and pointing nearly up; draw it to you in little jerks.
See Rising man.
See Begin.
See Done.
See Hide.
Hold hands open and curved, one on each side of the head, two or three inches away. A whiteman's sign, but quite well known now to the younger generation of Indians.
Sign He, Think, Strong.
Same as Way.

Lay points of both compressed hands on centre of breast, then spring them away, out and aside, turning them flat and palms up. Sometimes but one hand is used. I am inclined to think that this should be simply Heart and Behold, although none of my Indians made it that way. Sometimes sign You, Tell, True.
Sign Whiteman, Chief, Council (No. 2).

headword.note: Wink, that is, close one eye.
headword.sign: This ancient sign assumes that the person who should see, closes the eye next his accomplice. (Pop. Also Cheyenne.)

Sign Wolf (i.e., wisdom, analogous with our word “foxy”), then turn downward the points of the two fingers representing the wolf ears, back of hand near the eyes and moving the hand from right to left as in surveying the ground. (Scott.)
Hold the right “4” hand near the heart, pointing to left, rotate a little back and forth. (Blackfoot.)The Cheyennes use V hand, which makes it the same as If. See Because.
See Policeman.
See Scorn.
Use Glad or Sit, Good. (Blackfoot.)
Sign Go and Long Time. (Blackfoot.)
See Discussion, Speech, and Talk.
See Make and Food.
With both L hands make a horizontal circle, then holding left unchanged, put something into it with right and add Fire. Compare Centre and Hole.
Place flat right on flat left hand, palm to palm, then flip the right like a pancake, turning it palm up as it drops on the left.
As in the above Frying, but leave out the left hand entirely.
Sign Man, Makes, Food.
See Raccoon.

Hold out the left A hand, thumb straight and resting on index finger; place the ball of the thumb of right A hand on back of left thumb near its base; twist the right hand by wrist action to the right and downward until the right thumb slips off with a snap against the right index. Repeat once or twice.
Interlock the fingers, hold arms curved in front, horizontal; then add Enclosure, i.e., swing the hands apart and draw back each in a half circle till their heels meet near you.

headword.note: Sitting in a circle and talking.
headword.sign: Bring the A hands, back outward, well out in front of body, a little lower than the shoulders, little fingers touching; swing them apart and toward the body so they meet close to it, forming a horizontal circle; palms forward; then add Discussion.
See Advice.
See Numbers.

headword.note: Striking a dead body with the coup stick.
headword.sign: Strike the top joint of the right G index on the middle of the left G index, as the right is swung from below up. Grand Coup sign Coup and Great.
See Land.
See Count coup.

headword.note: Driving or rounding up in secret.
headword.sign: Thrust the right L hand back nearly up under flat left, held palm down, twisting the right by wrist action. Compare Sweetheart and Glitter.
See Fear.
Sign Wolf and Small.

Place base of wrist of right hand, palm down, on some flat surface, thumb and little finger extended and curved (others closed) to represent claws. Then move the hand sidewise backward, waving the claws. (Scott.) Compare Spider.
See Want.
Hold out the V hand level; draw it back, opening and shutting the V. Compare Dog.

Raise compressed right hand, all fingers together, tap the forehead with it and make one or two quick circles with finger tips in the air. (Sheeaka.) Tap the forehead, shake the head and point to the person. (Pop.)
Swing the 5 hand in horizontal circles near the forehead, going with the sun. Going the reverse way always raises a laugh among Cheyennes; it both intensifies and makes the idea ridiculous.
See Indian.
Sign Water, then draw right G hand, back up, held low, from opposite left side, past body to right side; finger level, pointing to left and waved sidewise. Compare River, Snake, Crooked, and Wire.
Sign Strong, Bad, Work.
Point the right G forward and slightly down; push it slowly forward in a succession of large, horizontal zigzags, each arched a little.
Rest the forehead low on the left hand. Or sign Heart, Bad.
Hold right G upright, left G at right angles across last joint of right.
See Across.
With right index make a little cross over the heart. This means “I give you my word of honor.” (Pop.)
Sign Bird and Black.
See Few.
See Indian.
See Exterminate.
See Yell.
With G forefingers near the eyes trace the courses of tears. If excessive, sign Rain from the eyes. (Blackfoot.) Compare Pity.
Make the sign for Wolf and add the sign Equal. Compare Consider.
Saw the lower edge of the flat right across the palm or edge of the flat left.
See Meat.
With flat right hand, back to the right and level with the right shoulder, make a spiral upward. A very small one for a little dust whirlwind, and a large violent one for a dangerous cyclone.
Sign for Stream or River and Hold. (Blackfoot.)

Bring the flat (or “5”) hands in front of body about height of breast, with fingers pointing nearly up, palms toward each other about six inches apart. Move the hands briskly upward and downward a few inches several times, simultaneously, mostly by elbow action. Compare People.
Hold up one flat hand face high, back to side, thumb raised and up: jerk up and down. (C)

headword.note: The Scout or Wolf going ahead and coming back to report danger.
headword.sign: Right V hand, back upward, moved directly and slowly forward in front of the right shoulder and then suddenly and quickly drawn back at the same time the body is thrown back a little. (Sheeaka.)
Sign Look, Little, Afraid (No. 2).
Sign Strong and Bad.
See Challenge.

Bring the flat hands, back outward, in front of face, right hand nearest face, left a little ahead, hands crossed, tips of fingers about opposite centre of forehead; bring the hands very slightly toward face. Compare Ashamed and Blind.
Sign Born and Female.
See Time.
Sign Little, Sunrise.
Hold right flat hand above left flat hand and in same plane; right little finger on left index then raise the right hand a few inches higher. (C)
Make the sign for Die and Sleep. (C) The Cheyennes use Die.
Press the palm of extended right hand slightly against right ear, and move the hand in small circle parallel to and close to the ear. Sometimes add Not.
See Owe.
Sign Give and Lie.

headword.note: Think, then act or settle it.
headword.sign: Lower the head and raise right fist to chin; then raise the head and cut down with the little finger edge of the flat right hand. (Sheeaka.)

This is a sentence rather than a sign. With 4 hand over heart, sign Consider; point slowly in two or three directions for Ways; sign That by vigorously swinging the right G index out, pointing down; then add So by raising it slightly up and then down. (Blackfoot.)

Hold flat right hand, palm down, high above, a little to the side of flat left hand, palm up; move them together in succession of little jerks. Sometimes for emphasis finish by compressing all right fingers to a point in the left palm. Compare Increase and Heap.
Hold up G hand, back to right, higher than shoulder; lower the hand several inches, at same time bend the index. (C)
Hold up the right G, shoulder high, with index bent in a hook; rotate as in Old.
See Act.
Hold the left flat hand horizontal, chin high, back up, fingers pointing to right; then drop right arm to full length down with flat right hand palm up, under left, fingers pointing forward.
Both hands fully spread, palms in and held up to the side of the head to represent the horns of a deer. (Sheeaka.) This is generic for Deer. Compare Elk.
Sign Deer then set compressed hand at each ear pointing up and forward to indicate the large ears, as in a mule. (Scott.) Compare Bear.

Raise the right G hand, pointing up and shoulder high; switch it from nearly horizontal right to nearly horizontal left and back, several times, pausing at the low point each time. Sign Deer if need for clearness.
See Abuse.
Sign the same as the first part of Corral, but swing the joined hands to left and right. So also to separate the hands means to Loose or Remove protection. See Hold or Protect.
Point the T hand toward the person. This is an extremely insulting challenge implying also the extreme of hatred and contempt. See Challenge.
See Mockery.
See Excite.
See Go.
Sign Talk and All—that is, swing the curved flat right hand, palm down, and to left, in a horizontal circle in front of the right cheek.
See Want.
See Exterminate.
See Decide.
Sign Medicine, Horns, and Tail. A purely modern sign.
Sign Night, Grass, and the Shimmer; i.e., pass curved right 5 hand, palm down, with a tremulous motion just above the grass and add Water. (C)
Hold left hand flat, fingers horizontal, palm to you, breast high, pointing to right. Hold right G hand pointing to left above and within; pass it down, under and up beyond, still pointing to left.
See Melt.

Hold up the flat right, pointing forward and a little up, back to right. Swing the whole arm a foot to right, forward and upward, without bending the wrist, then back to first pose and repeat; a much-used and very plastic sign. Compare Bad and No.
See Trouble.
Use flat right hand as a spade.
Sign White, Little, Money.
Sign Noon or Night, as may be, and Eat.
Point the G finger forward and down, swing it forward in a curve till it is about horizontal and pointing in the line intended.
See Earth.
Rub the tips of the 1st and 2nd fingers with the tip of the thumb, exactly as in Powder, and add Bad.
Hold out the flat left, palm in, then shoot compressed right, points first, over and down; then rub upper edge of left with palm tips of right to mean wiped out.
Look around and sign See, Not. Sometimes use Hide.
Sign That, True, I think, No.
See Learner.
See Sorrow.
Found out.
Hold out both flat hands, palmsup, level, six inches apart, breast high; swing both to left, then back to right several times, to signify the handing of words back and forth.
Make the sign for Talk alternately right at left and left at right.
Push both flat hands forward palms out, fingers up, and turn away head. Or Heart, Tired. (C) Sometimes use Scorn.
Sign Horseman, then separate and lower right V hand, points up. If the points of the A hand were down, it would mean “looking on the ground.” See Mount.
Sign Hear, Not. Compare Obey.
See Far.
See Sorrow.
See Melt.
Place flat hands palm to palm and make the action of diving.

Hold out flat left hand, back outward, bring flat right hand, back out, some inches in rear of and higher than left hand, pointing downward and forward; then shoot the right hand downward and outward, back of hand grazing under the left hand and up beyond. Compare Born.
Sign Marry, then swing the fingers apart horizontally. This is a legal divorce. When Abandon is used it means leaving one's mate without legal divorce.
See Work.
Sign Chief and Medicine. (C) The Cheyennes omit Chief.

Sign Man, then swing right G on the level a foot, and back six inches; then raise and strike with same G down on middle of the line. (Sheeaka. Apparently no other Indian knew it.) The Cheyennes sign Man and Work, or Man and Knows.
With V hand back up in front of body draw the double trail down and backward. Compare Wolf. Seger maintains that the idea here is the dog's ears point backward as he runs. See Bark.
Sign Money and One. This would vary with the country, the sign stands for the unit of currency.
Hold left hand flat, fingers touching, point forward, thumb up and with edge of right similarly held, chop down close past the tips of the fingers.

Hold up both fists at level of chin, palms down, middle knuckles of left touching ditto of right; jerk them a foot apart as though breaking a cord; swing them apart, down, along and up. This is also used as a Period at the end of sentence and I have seen it used for Bankrupt. It is a northern sign recently established among the Cheyennes. Compare Fat and Break.
Tap chest with tips of right flat hand, then swing the hand briskly and high to right, keeping palm facing the left. (Sheeaka.) Merely shrug the shoulders. (Blackfoot.) The Cheyennes sign Go ahead.

Hold up flat right hand, palm out, and forward at an angle; sharply shake the hand by wrist action back and forward (not sidewise), also sometimes the head. Compare Easy and Rub it out. Sometimes use Stop.
See under Want.

Sign Teepee or House, then hold flat left hand, thumb up, and lay flat right across the palm with little finger of the right as the hinge; swing it till it lies flat on left palm. (C) The Cheyennes sign House, then pull and swing an imaginary door.
Very slowly shake the head. (Pop.) Cheyennes and other Indians add—right hand held out palm forward and down, open and shaken; really a slow, slight Question sign. See If.
Point straight down with right G, lowering the same. (Blackfoot.) Some use flat hand for this. Compare Here.
See Sorrow.
Sign Sleep and See; keeping the eyes closed.
Pass the palm of the L hand over the part of the body to be covered. Compare Hat, Moccasin, Robe, etc.
The O hand to the mouth as in drinking. Compare Want.
in the abstract would be: Draw the hollow hand level to the mouth from slightly above and down past chin, fingers pointing nearly forward. This combines Water and Swallow. See Water. Compare Speech.
Hold up right G hand back to you, then lay left G across back to indicate liquor, then add Drink. (Sheeaka.)

headword.note: Sense of driving a herd, or running off a herd.
headword.sign: With L hands horizontal, opposite each other and same height, about an inch between tips of thumbs; move the hands simultaneously in the direction of the drive.
Hold both hands, backs out, as though holding reins, thumbs straight, with index of each around its point. See-saw the hands on the same level.
Sign, Long time, Rain, No.
Sign Water and Die.
With G fingers draw a large circle, beginning together well forward, each making half-circle, ending near body; then strike on it several times with right A.
Sign Crazy, Drink, and Much.
Sign Stream, Water, All gone.
See Thirsty.
Sign for Bird and make the broad bill with flat hand held under the nose, back up, pointing forward; push it forward a few inches, or else with thumb below and first and second fingers above.
Sign Cut and Not or Bad. Compare Saw.
See Dunce.
Press the finger tips of the flat hand on the mouth; add Talk and No.
Strike forehead with right fist knuckles. (D) A Cheyenne signed it Behind, Book, Know, Not.
See While.
Rub tips of right fingers with tip of right thumb, as in Powder; then with flat right hand horizontal, palm down, pat several times toward the earth. (Scott.) See Earth and Land.
Sign for That, Me, and Go (emphatically).
Right flat hand, face high, pointing up, twisted slowly from left to right two or three times. Compare Appear, Alive, and Doubt.
See Decrease.

The sign for Bird is slowly made, then with the G hand in front of the face, back to right, describe a downward curve from between the eyes to indicate the curved bill of the eagle. This same sign is given for Roman nose, but hold the back of the hand forward.
See Soon.
Sign Sunset and Little of. (C) The Cheyennes understood this, but preferred to swing the Sun sign down to near, but not quite, level.
Sign Little and Daybreak, or Sunrise.
Make a ring of each index and thumb and apply to each ear-lobe, backs of hands out, other fingers raised.
Sign All with both flat hands, palms down, then pat down with both hands twice. Compare Land.
Point down, then with thumb and finger tips, lift and rub some imaginary soil as in Powder.

Hold flat hand at face height, fingers half spread and a little curved, palm forward, leaning toward the person and gently and slightly shake the hand from side to side. (Sioux and Blackfoot.) With slight modification, much used on the railway in shunting. Compare Rub it out, Question, and Do not.
Throw the nearly compressed right hand lightly in a curve past the mouth several times. Compare Water and Drink.
Sign Eat, then lay L hand on breast, palm in, and raise it to opposite chin. Compare Hang.
See Result.
See Try.
Sign Bird and Born; then indicate the size of the egg with finger and thumb of one or both hands.
Hold out left V hand, back up; tap each finger in turn with right G. Compare Both.

Hold the hands above the head at arm's length on each side, thumb and first two fingers of each spread, others closed; jerk them forward two or three times for three or four inches. Compare Deer, Moose, and Caribou.
Place the right 5 hand, points up, in the left C hand, which is back, forward, and down; drop the right hand down out of the left, closing left to O. Compare All gone.
See Camp.
See Surround.
Hold out both 5 hands level, palm to palm, finger tips touching; swing them apart in a horizontal circle; draw them back; end with fingers wide apart and heels touching. A much-used sign. See Corral.
See Done.

Hold flat curved right hand close in front of breast, breathe heavily, swinging the hand near and from; then raise the right A hand in a curve out and up, about head high. (Sheeaka.) Understood, but not used by Cheyennes.
Sign Shake hands and Not. Sometimes use Hate.
Sign Prisoner, then with right index indicate a ring on ring finger of left hand. (D)
Sign Bye and bye, Marry. See Courting.
Swing the flat hands, palms up, up and down for six or eight inches, as in Road, but not progressing. Or, sign Road and Meet.
Sign Work and Soldier.

in general sense (Levelling off a full measure). Hold out left O hand, back to left, and across the top from right to left pass the flat palm of the open right hand. Sometimes add Strong, to mean Plenty. See Eaten enough.
Hold out hollow left, back up, and pass compressed right hand under and beyond.

Hold G hands in front of breast, side by side, backs up, pointing to front, about two inches apart; push them forward together and a little up in a sweep. A much-used sign. Compare Race, Parallel, Marry, and Mate.
Sign Wrong and Wipe out. See Rub it out.
See Free.
See Equal.
Sign Night and Little of. (C) See Early evening.
See Always.
Sign Work and Bad.

Close all fingers of right hand, except index and little finger; to point these at any one means “You have the Evil Eye.” In Italy this is an insult. (Pop.) In France it means “Shame on you,” or “I put you to shame.” (Pop.)
See But.

With right palm down, make a succession of curves, marking stages each higher than the last, beginning very low; then finish by swinging both hands, palm down, away up and forward. (Sheeaka.) Or sign Heap. Sometimes use Enough.
See Trade.

Sign Heart, then hold the 5 hand near the heart, back to right, pointing upward; raise it a foot shaking the fingers. The Blackfeet use this for Glad, and in most cases among the Cheyennes it implies pleasant excitement.
See Wipe out.
See Be.
See Hope.
See Describe.
Sweep the flat right hand, palm in, across the face and down. This is sometimes used for Person.
See Melt.
Signs for Die and Recover.
The right flat hand in front of breast, back up, swept briskly out, forward up and down, to rest palm up. Compare Another and Lie Down.
See Autumn.
Sign River or Creek, then hold out flat left, back out, and push flat right over it, bending right fingers over far edge, making them tremble.
Sign Work, Backward. (Blackfoot.) The Backward is indicated by holding the flat left well forward, drawing the flat right back in jumps.
See Lie.
See Glory.
Sign Hill, then over that hold right G, palm forward, index just above left thumb. Or, sign Chief and Brave.
Move the G hand forward and upward in a long slow arch, finishing at arm's length with down curve.
Flat right hand curved, pointing to left, back forward, pushed out and up at arm's length. Compare Close.
Sign Land and Planting.
Sign Corn and Chief.

Hold out the flat left hand, back to left. Bring the flat right hand, back to right, several inches in rear of and slightly to right of left, pointing to front and downward; carry right hand swiftly past left and close to it, and as it passes, by wrist action, raise the hand so that fingers will point upward and in front, making a curve; at the same time, draw back the left a little.

headword.note: Shape of a fat animal's hips.
headword.sign: Hold the fists out side by side, thumb to thumb, then swing them apart in two curves, up, out, down, and a little forward. Compare Done and Break.
See Oil.
With compressed right hand, pluck at the right breast two or three times, as though drawing out milk. Sometimes add Male. Compare Mother.
Sign Brother-in-law, Old, and Man.

With flat right hand, arm at full length, make a succession of short chops down. This is fault-finding with another. For “fault-finding with me” chop toward the breast. (Sheeaka.) See Abuse, Chop, and By itself. The Cheyennes use You, All time, Scold.
The head stooped down and the right arm thrown up, palm out, as though to ward off danger. (Sheeaka.) The Cheyennes understand, but prefer Woman, Heart. See Afraid.
Pluck an imaginary feather from left arm with index finger and thumb of right hand and blow it away from the mouth with one puff, at same time opening the finger and thumb to let it go.
Sign Wing, then make as though pulling out and holding up One feather.
Place the tip of the middle finger, others extended and lifted, against the heart; then draw it up a little way. (D)
The hand as above, but pushed forward and moved a little from side to side. (Sheeaka.) See Hot.
Hold out left flat hand, back up; press tip of right flat hand on it at various places. Compare At.

Draw the flat right hand, palm in close to the side of the head, finger tips about on line with the top of head; lower the hand, at the same time curving fingers as though combing with them the hair over ears and cheeks; finish with a snap at line of shoulders. Two hands are used in the north. Compare Comb. For White woman indicate an immense hat. (Sheeaka.)
See Bring.
See Small.

Hold loosely clenched A hands well out in front of body, thumbs toward body, about height of shoulders and about three inches apart; bring right hand in toward body few inches, at same time move left out about same distance; then carry right out and bring left in, repeating these two or three times, making them by wrist and elbow action. This is a fight of many; for a fight of two, use the G fingers same way. In old days, according to Father Isadore, the signal for Battle was a handful of dust thrown in the air. The gesture of doing this came to mean Fight.
Sign Look around here and there, then shoot out the V hand and pick up an imaginary something with finger and thumb. Compare Choose and Hunt.

Also called Bar up, King's X, King's Cross, Pax, Truce, Fins, Fines, etc. Hold up right hand with first and second fingers crossed. This means “I claim privilege, what I do now is outside the game.” (Universal in our schools and probably very ancient.)
Hold out flat left, palm up; rub flat right on it, palm down, making two sunwise circles. Also use Done.

Hold the right hand down and forward at arm's length, back in front of the body; fingers hooked so the tip of thumb is over the nails of first three fingers and holds them down; raise the hand six inches and spring the fingers upward, free, separate, and straight. Do this twice. Compare Aurora.
Hold up right thumb and index as though holding something and blow it steadily; add Fire if needed for clearness. Compare Feather (small).
Make the motion of striking a match on the side of the thigh and thrusting it forward.
Right hand in front of right shoulder; throw palm forward six inches and at same time straighten and spread all fingers with a jerk. Some add a hand clap or many for Volley firing.
Hold up left 5 hand, palm to right; push it forward, then tap the little finger with the right G. (Sheeaka. A Sioux sign, but understood by Cheyennes.) Compare Last and Guide.
Sign Ahead.
Make sign for Water; then hold flat right hand, back to right, in front of right shoulder, elbow high; and move the hand sinuously forward. Often omit Water. Compare Snake.
Sign Marten and Big (suggested, not established).
Lay low edge of flat right hand on upper edge of flat left near thumb base. Push right forward and down, turning left back up. (C) Or, sign Work and All right. Compare Mend, Council, and Law.
See Quandary.
Hold flat right well out, back to right. Lay left G on wrist of right. Wave right sidewise.
See Prairie.
See Meat.
Flat left hand, back up; place flat right on it, palm down, and move both to right as on waves. (C) Understood by Cheyennes.
Indicate source of Water, then hold up both flat hands, backs up, side by side, waist high; raise them neck high and sign Charge. Omit Charge unless needed.
Sign Bread and Powder.
Sign Grass, but higher; then clasp right index and thumb over left ditto, others closed; then turn the hands so the little fingers touch and the thumbs point up. (C)
Sign Grow; hold both compressed hands together in front, pointing up, backs out; spring them out into 5 hands, forming a circle pointing up.
Sign Bird, Small; then point here and there on the arm anywhere. Sometimes omit Small.
With flat hands held near shoulders simulate wings, beating exactly as in Bird, but move the hands forward a foot or more horizontally. Compare Bird.
Sign Water and hold the 5 hands, fingers crossed, in front of the eyes. (C) The Cheyennes understand this, but prefer the next:

Sign Turtle, then raise both 5 hands, palms forward, from the horizon up high; then spread them out. This expresses “turtles in the sky”; probably because the sky is clouded as is the water when the turtle moves in it.
Left G hand pointing nearly up, moved ahead and variously pursued six inches behind by right G hand. Sheeaka used his thumbs for this.
See Eat.

headword: Fool, Do you take me for a
headword.sign: With the right G index, draw down the lower eyelid a little; as though to say, “Can you see any green there?” (Pop.)
See Crazy.
Sign Crazy and Little of.
See Appendix.

Sign for Walk and for See with the fingers pointed down. This is Human tracks; for Horse tracks, sign Walk with the index and thumb of each hand in a three-quarter circle, other fingers closed, and then add See as here.
Sign Walk quickly, and Race.
Throw the flat right hand forward and palm down, slowly toward the object. Compare Stop, Give, and Wait.
Sometimes use Belongs. Hold out the two G hands nearly back up, a foot apart; swing them together till the G tips are about two inches apart. (Sheeaka.)
Lightly close the right as though it held some object, push it forward and upward, back to right. (Blackfoot.)
See Defend.

Hold both forearms upright, with all the fingers much spread, meaning Trees, the right hand nearest the body, its back against the palm of left; then separate them, drawing right hand toward the body, moving the left away and upward in a curve. See Tree.
See Always.
See Excuse.
Touch the forehead with the right N finger. Shake the head and motion as though to brush away an imaginary fly from near the nose. (Sheeaka and Pop.) See Remember not.
Clap right hand down on left (for surprise); lay right G, palm forward, on forehead, and add Hid.
See Ahead.
See Future.
Sign I Saw, then reach forward and with index and thumb pick up an imaginary object. Compare Choose.
At a distance, hold both hands high above the head, fingers closed, thumbs pointing toward each other. (Pop.)
Point forefinger at the person. (Sheeaka.) Or sign You, I, Understand.
See Animal.
Indicate the size of animal and bushy tail.
S hands crossed at wrists. With an effort, break the imaginary bonds and throw the hands apart, turning palm sides up. Sometimes add Go. See Pardon.

Hold flat right hand in front of breast, fingers forward, back down; move by wrist action sharply to left a few inches; then jerk back to place two or three times as though cutting something loose. Compare Chop and Wild Animal.
Sign for Cold, Water, then raise and move till the flat hands are touching in front, side by side, backs up and at arm's length. (C) See Ice.
Hold right N hand in front of neck, palm forward, pointing up; raise it head high, slightly advancing it. Alliance is sometimes used, or even Peace.
Water; then with compressed right hand make long hops forward.
Touch digit of right G hand to right shoulder, then throw it in a long sweep up and forward. Sometimes Indians use flat hand for this.
Sign, Cold, White, and Strong.
Sign Tree, then make a ring of right index and thumb, others closed, and place it here and there in the branches, and add Eat. Compare Berry, Leaf, and Money.
See Enough.
Make the signs for Time and Far ahead. See also Will.
Sign Ride, then hold out flat hands, palm to palm, right in advance; work them up and down together, the tips describing vertical curves. Compare Fast and Work.
Hold the hands out level, six inches apart; swing to left and right simultaneously, in easy curves down and up, keeping them near each other as though swinging a tom tom. Compare Drive and Herd.
Sign Mountains, then hold out left L hand, thumb pointing to left and upward, pass flat right between, touching at bottom.
Strike down with right G as in Here, but in several directions, then add Plant and Eat.
See Together.
Sign Heart and Big; or, Heart, Pities.
See Kind.
See Easy.

headword.note: That is, he gets, or obtains.
headword.sign: Reach out left hand, back up; then with right hand, grasp something on top of it; then close and draw back the right. If it is the first person who gets, finish by drawing the hand toward oneself. Sometimes omit left hand.
See Recover.

Bring right G hand in front of centre of body, pointing down; then draw it upward, as though drawing the forefinger out of the mouth, upward and to the front and at the same time exhale a breath. (Scott.) The Cheyennes sign Big eyes (as in Owl), and shaking the hands at the same time. See Spirit.

Hold left hand, hollow up; with right hand pick an imaginary object out of the left; carry it forward, at the same time turning right hand flat, palm up, and fingers pointing down and forward. Or, sign Give and Free (2nd).
Sign Female, Young.
Hold out flat right, back to right, shoulder high; swing it out and down.
Swing the hand from the person giving toward you. This, with the sign below, shows the beginning of a conjugation.

Bring the flat right hand well out in front of body, about height of neck, back of hand nearly to left, lower edge nearest to body, pointing upward; draw the hand in toward the body and a little down; at the same time, bend the hand and the wrist so the fingers touch the chest. Compare Tell me and Half-Breed.
See Abandon.
See Sorrow.
Sign Heart and Day.
Hold out the L hand with index pointing forward, back up. Rotate with a flash, or jerk so the thumb is upright and back to its original pose. Do this once or twice. See Courting and Sweetheart.
Sign Clouds, then draw them down near head. (C) Or, sign Thinking and Bad. Compare Cross and Angry.
Sign All, Good, Know. See Famous.
Hold up both 5 hands, palms forward, pointing up, at arm's length, nearly level; raise up very high and at the same time spread widely. A much-used and very plastic sign. May sometimes mean Weather.
Hold flat right hand, back to right, breast high, pointing to front and down; swing it up and out till the fingers point forward and upward. Often combined with Arrive there.
Hold up right G, palm forward; move it forward in short pushes.
Hold flat right hand in front of body, palm forward, fingers upright; move the hand to front in a slight curve to right; repeat once or twice. See Begin.
Sign Horns, with the straight G fingers on each side of head, and Beard. Sometimes omit Horns, signing only Beard.
Sign Medicine and Great.
Sign Money and Yellow, or Hard and Yellow.
See Absent.
Hold the flat extended right hand, back up, level, touching the left breast; swing the hand briskly out to front and slightly up in a curve.
Hold right hand flat, palm down, pointing forward horizontally at height of head and shake the hand up and down by wrist action. A whiteman's sign recently adopted by Indians.
Sign Day and Good.
Sign Bird, moving the wing hands slowly; hold both flat hands low, side by side, a little apart, backs up; push them down sharply a few inches, for flat feet. Sometimes also make the honk sound.
See Gap.
See Telltale.
With the flat right, pat one's own back where the baby is carried. Among the Cheyennes, only the grandmother uses this.
See Count Coup.
Sign Old and Father.

Sign Father, then hold curved left hand in front of body, palm in; and on outer side make the sign Father again. For Great grandfather, repeat the sign for Father once more. For Ancestors, many times. (Crow signs given me by La Forge.)
As above, but use Mother sign. (LaF.)
Sign Old, Mother.
See Keep.
Hold both hands low, palms up; turn all fingers upward straight and swing hands slightly apart. For Grass growing, raise the hands a little. Compare Children.
Use Thank you sign. Or else raise the flat right hand, palm forward and face high; draw it downward in a sweep several times as though stroking the other person's breast. For emphasis use both hands.

Hold out both flat hands, thumbs up, six inches apart, pointing level forward; draw them back level to you, sign Die; then hold out left flat hand as before and slap on it two or three times with the flat right, for cover up.

Hold out the slightly curved 5 hands in front of breast, few inches apart, palms toward each other, pointing to front; separate hands the right to the right and up a little and near the body; the left to the left and down a little and farther away. Compare Big and Wide. Sometimes use Much or High.
Sign Ahead, Great. So also Less than would be Behind, Small.
See Sorrow.
Sign Heart, Down. See Give up and Mourn.
See Land.

Sign Bird and show size; then with right hand low, palm up, fingers closed except first curved up like neck, imitate forward walking. (Sioux.) Sign Bird then indicate fast wing beats and whirring. (Blackfoot.) Sign Bird and Tail cut off. (Cheyenne.)
Hold right G hand low, back down, index finger pointing upward, in front of body; raise the hand by gentle jerks.
(Show trail and Lead.) Point with right index forward and downward; then add Lead. (Sheeaka.)
Hold up left 5 hand, little finger forward, and ahead of the right G, all moved forward in easy swings.
Make the motion of holding and aiming a gun; then add Fire-off. For Rifle, add the working of the lever.
See Fire.
Sign Gun and Powder.
With G forefinger draw a small circle on forehead, then sign Prisoner. (D)
Sweep the flat hands, palms down, back and forth; then strike three times down with the right G. (Sheeaka.) Or sign Way and Keep. (Seger.)
Hold out left G, draw right G back from left knuckles to shoulder; add Same.
Sign Rain, Cold, and with curved right index on thumb indicate size of stones.
Hold out left forearm horizontally in front of body and sweep the curved right 5 hand, palm up, along it from elbow to wrist. For Human hair, touch one's own hair. Compare Wing.

Hold left flat hand in front of breast, back out. Lay the lower edge of right flat hand on upper edge of left index, in middle, and jerk it toward the tips of left. Sometimes the right hand alone is used edge downward, drawn back and down as though its under side were cutting something. Compare Part.

With flat right hand pointing up, little finger next middle of the breast, palm to right, halve the body down breast, swing it far to left, return to centre, then swing far to right and give the tribal sign for each half. Or, give the first sign only; that is, the hand cutting in the middle.

Hold the right flat hand, palm outward and downward, in front of the body, pointing upward and to front, hand about height of shoulders; move the hand sharply to front and downward, stopping it suddenly. Sometimes merely raise the hand flat forward.
Same as Half; but jerk one part to right and one to left. (C)
See Beautiful.
Swing the right L hand over the head down to the neck, palm in; close the thumb and index, then draw it up to height of head, index pointing down. This is done in two graceful sweeps.
Hang right G index like a hook on straight left G index.
See Want.
See Luck.
See Glad.
Sign Die, Beyond, and Dwell.
Hold out the left hand flat, edge down, and strike the palm with the knuckles of the right two or three times.

Bring the L right hand, back outward, in front, close to, and a little above the head; lower the hand until thumb and index are about opposite the eyes; spread thumb and index, passing down close to forehead.
See Axe.

Hold up both hands opposite left side of face in pose for Fire-off, back of each to its side; spring both out as in Fire-off, to left side, turning the left very little, so that it shoots forward; the right shoots to the left. Compare Abandon and Scorn; also Defiance. Sheeaka used only Defiance sign.
See Possession.
to (Bound down). Sign Prisoner and Work. Or, That, Work, Strong.

First make Bird sign, then imitate the seizing of the prey with both feet hawk-fashion, by means of both hands placed in front of body opposite neck, palms outward, make grasping motion forward. (Scott.) Or sign Bird and with curved G indicate bill. See Eagle.
Make the sign Male; or point with the thumb, other fingers closed.
Touch head; then hold curved flat hand near and flirt the fingers from behind the thumb two or three times to show the throbbing.
See Well.
With curved hands show shape, beginning at the lowest points. Compare Many, Mound, and Much.
Move the right G hand (pointed forward) from behind the right ear, past the ear and forward. Compare Listen and Indian Nez Perc=C3=A9.
Bring the compressed right hand against the left breast, over heart and pointing downward.
See Hot.
Simply point up with right G, back to right and head high. Compare Heavens and Happy Hunting Ground; also, Tall.
Hold both flat hands palms down, tips touching, over and in front of the head; sweep them down sidewise in outline of the dome. Compare Clouds, Carriage, Hot Weather.
Hold flat hands, a few inches apart, at same level, backs down, in front of body and pointing to front. Raise them slightly and let them drop several inches, finishing low. Compare Light.
Hold left forearm horizontally in front of body, hand A, and place flat right hand under left forearm and lift it slightly. (Sheeaka; borrowed from the Deaf.) Or, sign Work and With.
See Possession.

Hold out left 5 fingers a little curved, level, far advanced, shoulder high, backs up and out; moved forward and a little down together. For Herd grazing hold right 5 over left 5 wrist; draw right back toward body in a slight arc up and down.
See Drive.
Swing the right G, back up, from pointing up, to forward and down; then stab toward the ground two or three times. Compare Down, Earth, Place.
See Doer.

Swing the flat or else compressed right hand, palm down, under the flat left hand held in front, palm down; the left hand rests on the right at end of sign. (Sheeaka.) Sometimes add Hush. Compare Enter and Night.
Hold extended left hand, back up, in front of body, and with the right thumb and forefinger gather up, with a pinch, the loose skin on the back of left hand. Sometimes sign Robe.

Hold the flat right hand, back nearly up, pointing to front, in front of right shoulder; raise the hand according to the height intended. If it refers to humans, hold the right hand (compressed or G) vertically pointing upward. (C) To show height of small objects or animals, hold the flat left, palm up, under right. See Tall.
See Superior.
Push right A fist, back out, straight up, face high. For plural use both hands moved alternately up and down at different places. Compare Ridge, Lump, and Mountain.
See Possession.
Hold up flat left, palm forward, and sketch on palm with right G; then sign Past. (C) Or, Sign Long, Past, Tell me.
See Count Coup.
Sign I, Make, Him, Wolf. (Seger.)

Indicate size, then with a circle of two thumbs and two index fingers in front of mouth show muzzle; last, with flat right hand, or flat fist back up, make a snout and root. Usually give only the last sign.
Arms in a level circle, flat right hand inside flat left and overlapping, both palms toward you; swing to right and left. Compare Defend, Keep, and Corral.
Form a circle with thumbs and fingers of L hands. If need be, for clearness, hold left hand in position and pass the compressed right through the imaginary hole. Compare Cook and Boil.
See Yell.
Sign My, Teepee.
Sign Heart, Want, and Teepee.
Point right G straight forward from near throat; push it down and up in a slight curve till it is upright, then push it forward in two or three little jerks. See also True.
With right index draw a small cross on the heart. (Pop.)
Sign Heart, Want.

Hold the flat left hand, back to front, before face, fingers level; move it to right; and then, for a moment, place the first and second fingers of right astride it, as in Ride. Some omit the last gesture. The movements of the hand indicate the height, action, and speed of the horse.
Sign Man and Ride. Or, place the V right astride of the flat left and push both to right.

Hold the hand as in Feel; touch the middle right finger tip to tongue; reach it forward and down, as though touching a hot iron, quickly jerking it back. (A northern sign understood by Cheyennes.) The use of the middle finger is general, no doubt because it is longest.
Hold the 5 hands a few inches apart, backs up, above and in front of head, pointing toward each other. Lower the hands to level of face.
Draw right index crooked across the brow, left to right, as though to run off the sweat, others and thumb closed.
See Restaurant.
See Time.
Indicate the double slope of the roof by flat hands, pointing nearly up and joined together at the tips. Some use only the next sign for House.
Bring the hands in front of body and interlock the fingers near tips, fingers at nearly right angles and horizontal. Some add Roof by joining tips of flat hands as in House. Compare Basket.
The Indian salutation is sometimes used with hand salute; that is, all fingers closed except index and middle, as in Friend.
See Question.
See Question.
See Question.
Hold up both 5 hands, palms forward, opposite right shoulder; thumbs nearly touching; swing to left and down. Precede this with one for one hundred, two for two hundred, etc.
Draw the lower edge of flat right hand, back down, across the stomach; emphasize by drawing back and forth.

headword.note: In the sense of seeking for.
headword.sign: The fingers of the right V hand brought near the eye, but pointing forward; then swing horizontally from side to side, the eye looking wherever they point. Compare Choose, Find, and Look. Or, shade the right eye with the right hand and swing the head. (Pop.)
Hold out both G hands, one behind the other; swing in up-curves forward and sidewise, but always one behind the other.
Jump the flat hands up and down before you, quickly, palms up. Compare Light, which is similar, but slower.
Make sign Male and Marry.
See Silence and Halt.
Sign Wolf and Like. Or Face and Two. According to Ruggles, the Paiutes sometimes use this second combination for Liar; the Cheyennes understand it but do not use it.
For I touch the centre of the breast with the right thumb extended, other fingers closed. This and the next are used indiscriminately by the Cheyennes.
Sign Water and Hard. Sometimes add Cold.
Sign Water and Cold; then hold up right G, pointing downward and dropped a little.
Lay the right G on the forehead, pointing up, palm to left; swing it down to horizontal at mouth level, then push it straight forward and up in a curve. Compare So.
Sign Work and Not.

Lay the right V hand on the heart, pointed down and to left front; rotate the hand on the wrist so the back is alternately up and out. When many emotions (i.e., perplexity) are to be expressed, use all fingers extended. See Consider.
Shrug shoulders and raise one flat hand. (Pop.) Or, sign Know, Not. Compare Forget.
Sign the verb in question, then strike the flat right, palm down, onto the flat left, palm up. Or add Push. (C)
