Sign Can and Not. See also Cannot. The Cheyennes use True, Not.
See Arrest.
Make a semicircle of the left arm held out level; then drop the compressed right hand downward between the left and the body. Outside begins the same, but the right drops outside the left.
Make a horizontal ring of the left index and thumb, then drop the right index down into it. For a house, use Enter.
Sign Push, Talk, and Go; that is, try to talk into going.

Hold out the flat hands, palm to palm, well apart at same height; separate them more and more in slight jerks; or, if it is more explicit, do it with the flat hands one above the other. See Add to. Compare Decrease.
With the tips of right flat fingers, rub the side of the flat left held out back up, in short strokes; meaning reddish, because all men's hands out west are red at this place. Compare Smooth.
Indicate the eagle feather at back of head. (Sheeaka.) Or sign Man and Red. (Scott.)
Give the Tribal sign and add All in each case.
Tribal signs:
Draw the right G finger up and down along the left G several times, from near the point to the base, a foot long each stroke. Compare Poor.

With all fingers of right hand compressed so the points are together, tap the left breast, that is, sign Mother. As Sherman Coolidge tells me, the Arapahoes claim theirs to have been the mother of all tribes. In the south, the sign is rub the side of the nose with the right G, referring to their one-time salutation of nose-rubbing.
Sign for Lodge and Bad. (C)
Sign for Moccasin and Black.
Draw the right N over the left N from the tips back to the knuckles (because they wore pants). (Father Isadore.)

headword.note: Perhaps meaning “striped tail,” because they used turkey feathers; or, more likely, “finger-choppers,” because they chopped their fingers when in mourning..

note.sign: See Ojibwa.
Sign for Snake, pushing the hand forward. Compare Shoshoni. (Seger.)
Sign Rabbit, People; but usually omit People.
Sign Bird slowly. Sometimes only one hand. Or hold S hand, palm forward, at brow for “Pompadour Indians.”
See Sioux.
See Koutenais.
Sign Big Belly.
Sign Dance and Snake.
With the tips of the flat right, palm up, back to left, describe an upright ellipse near the right ear; because they used to cut their hair on that side to show the ear ornaments or ear painted red.
Sign for White-tailed Deer. (C)
With right hand compressed so all tips touch, tap the chin and jaw. (C)
Sign for Work, Blanket, and Striped.
Pass index of right G hand level under nose from right to left. Compare Hear.
Sign Tree and People; or sign Paddle, People. (Blackfoot.)
Rub the flat right hand, palm out, over the side of the head and down, as though shaving the head; the little finger being the sharp edge.
Sign Rabbit, Robe.
Hold up the right V hand, palm forward, near right ear; then swing finger-tips forward.
Rotate half closed hand, palm in, near right cheek.
Sign Two Quivers; that is, Arrow, Two; then drop compressed left once or twice into C right hand.
Same as Osage sign.
Sign for Snake and sometimes add Bad, Lodge.
Draw the right G finger across the throat.

Sign for Sioux and Encamp, then make an incomplete circle with index fingers and thumbs; then, holding left in pose, strike last joint of index with tip of right G and similarly right index with tip of left G. (C)
Sign Black and Red.
With right G finger tap the forehead in a circle.
Sign for Sioux and Nez Perc=C3=A9. (C)
See Agent.
Both hands held down by the thighs; at the same time a shrug of the shoulders. (Pop.)
Sign Soldiers and Walk.
Hold up both G fingers, the one representing the inferior much lower.
For several inferiors use the 5 hand to represent them, while the G of the other hand up high represents the superior.
See Dwell.
If with reference to another person, make sign for Work and Bad. If with reference to one's self, make signs Do, to me, Bad.
See Clean-handed.
See So that.
See In.
See Question or Query.
See Come between.
See Sick one.
See Searching; also Consider.
See Metal.
Sign Land, then with L hands make a horizontal circle; hold left hand in pose, sign Water with right hand; then compress it and draw it point down around the circle just made. Compare Lake.
Scratch the left palm with nail of the right G index; or else the thigh.
Hold the fists near the breast; alternately swing each elbow out and back a little. (C)
Hold out flat left, back up, and with right G stab under it once or twice.

The right G finger upright and crossed on top with the left G; this cross then placed above the eyes, the face looking upward; then the cross pushed toward the sky to the full extent of the arms. The real meaning being, “He who was crucified and is now in heaven.” (Scott.) Or sign Big, Medicine, Child.
Sign Play; that is, hold the right 5 hand near the mouth, back down, fingers a little curved; swing the hand forward and upward; then add Talk.
Sign Heart, Glad, and Sing. (C) Sign Heart, Happy, or Playing.
See Consider.
Stand right V on left palm; assume these to be legs and make them jump up and down. (Sheeaka.)
Hold compressed right hand, pointing to left, near right shoulder; swing it up forward and down in a long curve. Also used for Animal, in which case use several short jumps.
See Younger; also Inferior.

From a position near each side, bring the extended O hands, palms inward, together so the tips of right thumb and index touch tips of left thumb and index, like two balance pans side by side. (D) Or, sign True and Same, or Good.
Make the sign Yes once or twice; or else, use True.

Hold out flat right hand, back to right, fingers level; hold up left G hand, back to left; swing them together; grasp left index in closed right hand and move the hands slightly to right and left. Compare Hold and Remember.
Sign Good and Near. (C)
Sign Stop and Sit down, or simply Stop. See Hush.
Indicate the shape of the mouth with both L hands; then, holding left unchanged, pass the right G into it; then, with right, lift as if by a handle. Some omit the “handle.” Compare Basket and Cook.
Hold up the two compressed hands, then swing them back against the kidneys.

Hold the right A hand, back nearly up, in front of shoulder, back of hand making a slight angle with wrist; strike to the front, downward and little to left, stopping hand suddenly and giving it slight rebound. For Kill me or Beat me, make the sign toward one's self.
Sign Brother and Distant or Near, as the case may be.
Sign Heart and Good.
See Fins.
(Whittling a stick.) With right A hand make motion of whittling the index finger of the left G hand. (Blackfoot and Pop.) The Cheyennes sign Cut and Bend.
Thumb up straight, rest of fingers closed, hand at side. (Pop.) Compare He, Turn down, and Opossum.

Hold left hand near mouth, then with lower edge of flat right make as though cutting off a piece of meat held between the left hand and the teeth. Understood but not used by Cheyennes. They indicate length with right G on flat left back and add Cut.
Sign Know, as below.

note.note: See, it is in my heart.
note.sign: Lay the right L hand, back up, on heart; swing it out, up in a slight curve, and down a little, palm up.
Shrug the shoulders and shake the head and raise the right hand open, palm up, to level of shoulder, inclining the head to the side. (Pop.) Compare Don't care.

note.note: Perhaps “I smell a rat” is the verbal form of the sign.
note.sign: Lay the right index on right side of nose. (Pop.)
Tap the forehead and add Big. Compare Conceit.
See Work.
Sign Water and with both L hands make an incomplete horizontal circle, then bring wrists together and swing finger tips apart. The last gesture is to suggest Wide. Compare Island.
Right A hand out in front, back up, moved forward a little and at the same time jerked down by wrist action and to left; repeat. Compare Old and Kill.
Pat toward ground with one or both flat hands; then swing them apart, flat, palms down, on a broad upward sweep left and right.
Sign Enclosure, but make it perpendicular; then in the same space sign Fire twice.
See Big and High.
Sign Rope, then hold right L hand over right shoulder, back nearly to right, index pointing up; throw it forward, upward, and down, close index and thumb and jerk the hand back. Often omit Rope.
Hold up the left 5 hand, thumb nearest you; push it straight away, then tap the thumb with the G of the other hand. (Sheeaka.)
Hold up 5 left hand and, away behind it, the G right. (Blackfoot.) Compare First and Guide.
Sign Winter, Beyond.
See After.
Hold both curved 5 hands, palms up, near the sides, a foot apart; then shake them up and down. Compare Play.
Sign Write, then Across two or three times. Compare Council (No. 2.)
Whiteman, Law, and Know.
Shake the head, throw it back, then drop both nearly open, hands limply, one held out at each side motionless. Compare Weak and Tired.
With right hand fingers grasp the flat left hand and drag it forward. (Sheeaka; borrowed from the Deaf.) Sometimes used also for Teacher.
Hold right A hand high in front of right shoulder, back to right; move it forward by gentle jerks, as though leading a pony. See Guide.
Sign Tree, then shake right hand shoulder high, with thumb and index pointed nearly up, forming an incomplete circle, others closed. Compare Money and Fruit.
Sign Book, then follow the lines with the right G finger and last draw it to Me. Or sign Book, Look, Know, making the last sign once or twice toward the Book. See Lesson.
Draw the L hands, backs out, one on each leg from well down to near hips.
Sign Give you (or me) and By and By, Give me (or you). Or, Give, Little while.
Hold the open flat hands a foot or two apart, palm to palm, one above the other. Hold the lower hand stationary and then draw the upper in jerks down toward the lower. See Decrease.
See Abandon.
Indicate size of letter, then close right hand, thumb extended; wet thumb end on lips and press on open left palm.
See Good, also Prairie.
Make sign for Lie; then indicate the person. To add the intensive Very much makes it equivalent to the strong and unprintable English expression that is used in extreme cases.
See Generous.
Sign Prisoner, throw the hands up and apart, then add Go. (C) See Pardon.
Hold the right V hand, back out, a little in front and to the right of mouth, and pointing to left; move the hand to left, past mouth, and downward.
See Alive.
See Day.

Hold out both flat hands, palms up; raise briskly together in one or two jerks. Compare Heavy, which is the same in pose, but in which the hands drop briskly. See Hurry, which is similar but much faster and raised each time in one movement.
With right G index held high, make a quick zigzag downward.
Sign Want.
See Equal.
Cover both ears with the hands and shake head. (Pop. and Blackfoot.)

Hold right L hand back to right near and around right ear; rotate the hand by wrist action (Cheyenne). Or hold hollowed right hand behind the right ear. (Popular sign understood by Cheyennes.) Compare Hear.

Hold right hand in front of body, shoulder high, back to right, end of thumb pressing against under side of index so that only about half an inch of index is seen beyond the thumb, other fingers closed. For emphasis, point at right with left G.
For small animal, person, etc., indicate the stature by holding out flat right, palm down, above the ground or above the flat left, palm up. See Boy.

Hold the right fist above the left as though both were grasping a thin stick, backs out. Twist the right hand and draw it in a little to the breast, turning it so the palm is a little upward instead of a little downward. Compare Few. Note Strong is its analogue.
See Alive.
See Dwell.

Compress the right hand, bend it much at the wrist, hold it so the back is forward and down, fingers level and pointing back; lay it on the right side under the ribs, draw it to the middle of the body following under side of ribs.
Against flat left palm turn thumb and index of right as a key.
Fully extend the left arm forward and downward, hand flat, palm down; lay right G finger on the left wrist; then draw it up to the shoulder. Often use Big.
Sign Heart and Want; or Heart, Want, and See.
Slowly draw the hands very far apart as though pulling out a piece of gum or elastic. See Time.
Make V hand and point with fingers in line of sight. Compare Hunting and See.
Point with forefinger at object and add See. Compare There.
See Warning.
See Liberate.

note.note: Hid, find, and not; apparently referring to the game of hide in the hand.
note.sign: Hold out both fists together, palms up, for Hid; then look about and point with right G hand behind here and there and to one side; add Not. (Sheeaka.)
The Cheyennes use Hide, which see. Compare Night.
Sign Look, Way, See, Not. (Blackfoot.)
See Fond.
Hold flat right hand low, back up.

Extend both G hands in front, palm side up; turn them in toward each other till the backs are up; forefingers still extended. Then add Good or Bad. (D) This suggests the gambling sticks in the Indian game. See Of.
Sign Medicine, or shake the head and the flat hand in front of the neck, then swing right G in a curve till it strikes the breast. (Sheeaka. A doubtful Sioux sign.)

note.note: Apparently “hill” that can be held in the hand.
note.sign: Hold up the right A hand, waist high, palm to you, thumb pointing up, wrist bent so arm is nearly level. Compare Hill.
Sign Noon and Eat.
Hold the 5 hands on the breast, one at each side, and indicate slow heaving. Compare Sick.

With the C hand at each side of the face indicate ruffs; with rings of index fingers and thumbs, show the eyes; then on the G right with the G left show the bob tail. (Sheeaka.) This is a description rather than a name.
Sign Cat and Short tail. (Scott.) The Cheyennes, who do not know the true Lynx, sign Animal and Short tail.
See Angry and Crazy.
Hammer the top of one fist with the other two or three times, giving both hands a twisting motion. (Sheeaka. Adopted from the Deaf.) Compare Work, which is mostly used by Indians for Make.
See Decide.

note.note: The one alone; the erect one.
note.sign: Right G held up at height of chin, palm forward; many Cheyennes make it back forward. For Boy sign Man then drop the hand down to a level that indicates height, and turn it palm to you.
See Indian Tribes.
Give signs for Country, River, Red (suggested).
See Way.
See Doer.
Hold up both hands, face high, half closed, palms forward; throw them forward in 5 shape. Repeat several times. See also Heap and Much.
Hold out left arm level, hand flat palm in; tap it a few times with right G hand from near wrist, moving each time an inch or so toward elbow. Compare Cheyenne.

note.note: Side by side, united as one.
note.sign: Sign Trade, that is, Bargain; then lay right G beside left G touching, both pointing forward level, not moved. (C) The Cheyennes omit Trade. Compare Equal, Parallel, and Mates.
Sign Weasel; that is, curve right G and push it forward, back up, in bounds; indicate size, yellow throat; then, running up a tree. This is a suggested description, as I found no established sign.
Strike an imaginary match on right thigh, or left arm.

note.note: One teepee and side by side.
note.sign: The G fingers in tent form, then up side by side, touching. (Sheeaka.)
Sign Friend, Same.
See Perhaps.
Touch one's own chest with the tips of the compressed fingers of the right hand. (Sheeaka.) Compare I.
For Breakfast, sign Eat and Sunrise; for Lunch, sign Eat and Noon; for Dinner, sign Eat and Sundown.
Sign Want and Say.
Sign Heart and Few.
See While.

headword.sign: Make a circle of right thumb and index (other fingers closed); lay it little finger in, on the centre of the breast. (C) Compare Policeman.

headword.note: In the sense of Sacred Mystery.
headword.sign: Hold V right hand close to forehead, palm forward, pointing up, separated; move the hand upward, twisting it so that the tips of the extended fingers will describe a spiral curve. This is hardly translatable.

Hold out left C back to left and pour into it from a bottle in right C hand. If a powder, pour on flat left palm. If a pill, pour on palm and afterward pick up with index and thumb. (Pop.) Sign Medicine and Eat. (Cheyenne, recent.)
Make signs for Man and Medicine.
Hold forefingers of both G hands a foot apart, pointing up, left farthest off; move together till touching at the tips. Compare Trade, Mistake, and Avoid.
Incline the head slightly forward and rest forehead on left hand, left forearm close to body. Understood by Cheyennes, but they prefer Heart on the Ground.
Hold up both “5” hands six inches apart, fingers pointing up, palms toward you; let the hands drop and slide aside, gradually assuming compressed position, backs up. (Sheeaka.)
Sign Wipe out, but slide the right palm over the left toward the tips in small jerks, then over and beyond.
Head hung forward; right A hand dropped a foot under chin; then sign Time, Back. (Blackfoot.)
Sign Heart and Know.

Lay the right G along to overlap the left G, then hammer on the left with right fist. (Sheeaka.) Note, this is mending iron or wood; to mend clothes, lay one index on other as above, then with right sew over edge of left. (Blackfoot.) The Cheyennes usually sign Make and Good. Compare Fix.
In the Roman Arena, the appeal for mercy was made by stretching the hand with first and second fingers raised and touching; others closed. (Pop.)
See Pity.
See Pity.
See Glad.
See Speech.
Sign Hard and describe or point to a piece of the metal in question. Or sign Strong, Hard.
Sign Star and with hand up high sign Fire and let it drop in a wavy line across the sky.
Sun and Straight up.
Hold out the left G hand, finger level. Drop the right G hand down onto it at the middle joint. Compare Half and Centre.
Strike down with lower edge of flat right between the fingers of left V held pointing up.
Hold up three fingers of left, tap the two outer with right G, then bend the middle one down.
Sign Night and Middle.

Hold out closed left at arm's length in front of shoulder, back up, index and thumb joined at top; drop it six inches, point down; then place index and thumb of similar right against it and swing right out level to right for two or three feet and drop it as before.
With both S hands make motion of milking.
Die and Way, and sweep the right hand high across the sky to show where.
See Mix.
Sign Water, Creek, and Animal. (Blackfoot.) Sometimes indicate size.

See Time. Hold left O hand horizontal for Watch; tap around it with right G finger for hours; then for minute add One, Small, and Time. Second would be the same with added signs Few or Very. (Sheeaka.)
Sign Small.
Hold right 5 hand high, opposite left shoulder, back out, pointing to left; move it horizontally to right with a tremulous motion. (C)
Flat right, points up, opposite face, shaken a little forward and back as though adjusting the distance. Sometimes sign Look first.
Sign Way, then thrust right G to right and to left, but not straight. Or sign Tell, Straight, Not.
See Avoid.
See Fog.
Hold up left G hand, then pass it by with right G hand.
Hold up the 5 hands, face high, points up, palm to palm touching; rotate one against the other. If in cookery, imitate the movement of stirring. Compare Play.
Pass the open hands over feet from toe to ankle. Usually but one hand is used, and it need not touch the foot.
Spread the right hand with fingers straight, point of thumb to point of nose, little finger toward the enemy. (Pop.) Not Indian originally, but all understand it now.
Cover the eyes with one flat hand, its fingers well apart. (Pop.) See Ashamed and Fog.
With right thumb and index, others closed, make a horizontal circle. Compare Sun, Fruit, and Leaf.
Sign Writing and Money.
Draw the flat left hand edgewise between the thumb and fingers of the right; then on left palm indicate shape. (C)
Sign Face, Man, Dog, Goes.
Sign for One and Moon and Die.
Sign Moon and Snow.
Sign Moon and Hunger.
Sign Moon and Crow.
Sign Moon and Short grass.
Sign Moon and Planting.
Sign Moon and Rose.
Sign Moon and Lightning.
Sign for Moon and Red.
Sign Moon and then draw a bow as in hunting.
Sign Moon and Leaf-falling.
Sign Moon and Mad.
Sign Moon, Night, and Long.

headword.note: Horns in the sky, or crescent.
headword.sign: Close right hand except thumb and first finger, which forms a half-circle or crescent, held above the right ear, back of hand forward. Sometimes expressed as Night and Sun.
Hold up flat hands for horns; but swing both backward and forward to indicate width; then, with both “L” hands, indicate the hanging muzzle. (Blackfoot.)
Hold out the flat left hand, palm up; then with right make as though throwing sand on it more and more, three times. (Sheeaka.) Compare Most. See Repeat, Add to, and Ahead.
Both hands palms down, flat, near together. Sweep them up, out, and apart; turning the palms up. The same as Day.
Hold out both arms level, full length, side by side, hands flat, backs forward, tips touching; raise them slowly to half height.
With right index and thumb make as though pricking right cheek with a thorn; then slap the place with right palm.
Sign More, then raise the right flat hand high above it. (Sheeaka.) Or sign Ahead, All.

With the finger tips of the right hand, make as though drawing milk from the left breast; add the sign for Female if necessary. (Scott.) Note the left breast for Mother, nearer the heart; the right breast is for Father.
Sign Brother-in-law, Old, Woman.
Make signs for Wheels, Horse, Not. Or sign Fire twice then make as though holding and turning steering wheel.
Flat hands together, backs up; separated and swept down to show shape. Compare Heap, which is just the reverse; also see Box.
Hold right V to right side pointing up; swing it up to right, over and down, points down, onto flat left, which is held thumb up. Or, omit left. (Blackfoot.)
Hold up A hand as in Hill, but higher, and add Hard. Use both hands alternately up and down in various places for plural. Compare Hill and Lump.

Sign Cat, Long tail, and Jump. (C) Or with both L hands indicate large round tracks; then, with right G, pointed down, raised and down, each time, indicate the zigzag trail. (Paiute sign, given me by C. B. Ruggles.)
See Gap.
With the flat hands used like knives, backs up, little fingers as though sharp edges, make as though to cut off the hair at each side of the head, and add Cry.
Sign Animal, with very small jumps, indicate size and Night; then, with right forefinger and thumb, nibble at left G forefinger.
Both G hands in front, backs out; lay right forefinger on left at middle joint, crossing at an angle of 45 degrees, and push both hands forward in slight jerks, each jerk a day's journey.
Sign Grass and Knife; the last at right lower than waist. Add Wagon if needed to indicate horse mower.
Hold curved 5 hands, palm to palm, a foot apart; swing down together and up face high, closing them; swing a little apart at the finish.

note.sign: See Excessive.
See Soft.
Hold extended hands alongside of ears, palms to front, fingers pointing upward; by wrist action, flop the hands to front and rear, representing motion of mule's ears.
Sign Kill and Free. Meaning an unjustified killing.
See Have to; also Begin or Push. See Imperative.
Sign Beaver and Small.

Hold out right G, back up, nearly level, pointing forward and to left; shake it, draw it to right; then hold up compressed right hand, back up, pointing forward, and grasp right wrist on lower side with left hand. (Blackfoot.)
Sign Possession; that is, hold out the A hand, thumb straight up; then swing it in till it touches the breast; or sometimes lay the spread flat hand on the breast. (Sheeaka.) See I.
Touch breast with point of compressed right, then sign Possession.
See Medicine.
Sign Called. “What is your name?” would be Question, You, Called.

With right forefinger and thumb, others closed, make an incomplete circle and lay it palm down against the palm of the flat left hand, held forward, back to left. Compare Brand and Called. Scott gives it as illustrated.
Press the thumb tip of the A hand against the palm of the flat left. Compare Letter.
See Called.
Hold flat hands out, palm to palm, about six inches apart, fingers level and pointing forward; move them toward each other until but one inch apart. Compare Fast, Between, Few, and Road.
Hold up both 5 hands, swing them toward each other and away in a great circle. (Sheeaka.) Not well established.

Sign Thirteen, Fires. This was once used on the Ohio, referring to the Council Fires of the thirteen original colonies. Then add Country by patting the ground with both flat hands and swinging them apart and up.
Touch the coat; then with fingers brush the cheeks for red.
Shake the coat lapel with right hand. (D) The Blackfeet draw a long line east and west then with flat hand point to indicate all north of that line.
Indicate long tail by signing Rope at back of head.
Place thumb of Y hand at the mouth, draw it down and outward to indicate the long pipe. (D)
Sign White Man, Far. (Blackfoot.)
Hold out the F hand well toward the left, palm up; draw it across in front of self from left to right, turning it palm down. (D) To sign Hairy Man would translate the nickname “Poilu.”
Cross the wrists of both 5 hands, thumb palm against thumb palm, and work the fingers. (D)
Land, Coat, and Red.
Same as Dutchman.
See Indian on p. 106.
Hold out left S hand, back up; swing right V around it and end with V resting on back of left. (D) Green Island Man would be more acceptable.
Sign Country and Rising Sun. Or sign Man, Short, Eyes, Oblique. The last by pushing up the outer corners of the eyes with G fingers. (These are suggested.)
Placing the fingers of the bent 5 hand on the chin, draw them down and off, letting the hand assume the flat position as it leaves the chin. (D)
With the little finger of I hand trace a cross in the centre of the forehead. (D)
Sign for White Man and Beard. In this case, Beard is made by rotating and jerking the 5 hand before the chin, points up.
Sign White Man, Black face. (C) Sheeaka also fumbled the open right over the knuckles of the half closed left, to indicate kinky hair.
Make signs for Country and Lakes. (Suggested.)
Arms akimbo, i.e., C hand on each side of the waist. (D) The Cheyennes call them Ride Easy from the Cossack circus performers; but it is not an established sign. Bear Man is suggested.
Cross the fingers of the right 5 hand with those of the left 5 hand on the chest, then let each drop to continue the lines. (D)
Sign Old, Mexican.
With G right hand draw first finger across the brow. (C) For illustration see White Man among W's.
Same as Close, which see.
See By.
See Next.
See Must.
Make the sign for Sew. (C)
Sign Close and Dwell, or Sit, Close.
Sign Brother's (or Sister's) Son. (C)
Right elbow fixed at side and with right G hand out straight, describe a complete upright circle, left to right; then throw the hand down to right in Not. (Sheeaka; probably borrowed from Deaf.)
See Rub it out.
Sign Little time, Traded. Or, Little while, Made, Good.
With right G struck down to right and left (for this and that) and Hear.
Sign Writing and Tell (i.e., Talk) to right and left.
See Neighbor.
Hold out the flat 4 left hand, palm down; with right G draw the left index toward the right; then tap the middle finger of left with right index. Or sign Close.
Bring the flat hands, backs up, well apart, out in front of body, breast high; move them together in outline of a dome, finally resting right wrist on left. Compare Hide.

Hold flat right hand, back up, in front of body, fingers pointing level and nearly forward. Swing the hand in a graceful curve to right and front, at the same time turning it thumb up, finishing with the back of hand to right and downward; the hand is swept into its position on a curve. Usually abbreviated into a short jerk of the flat hand to right, its palm kept facing left. Compare Bad and Different.

headword: Go away, the matter is ended
note.sign: Right G hand upraised to level of face, palm out, index upright; wave the finger hand from side to side by wrist action. Chiefly used by Cheyennes when joking. It is general in Latin countries. Compare White-tailed Deer.
Turn the flat palms forward, one near each trouser pocket. (Pop.)
See Midday.
See Tell.

Hold up right G, back to right and pointing upward about eight inches in front of the face; and, without stopping, carry it a little to front; then stop and give a slight rebound. Sometimes look up to make it clear that it relates to time of day, that is, the sun's course. Often it means this as in this day; this moment; this night.
Up to ten as shown on the fingers at the foot of previous page and this.
Or, with the thumb of the same hand, touch each of the finger tips in succession. (Popular and sometimes used by Cheyennes.)
Point at or indicate the person or thing; then turn down the first finger for 1st and the second finger for 2d and so on. Also see First, Next, and Last.
Hold the left 5 hand, palm up, fingers a little bent; with right G turn one or two left fingers down on left palm.
Sign Woman, Black, Hat.

Tap the chest with the tips of the flat right hand, then hold it at head height, palm forward. (Sheeaka.) Sometimes point to Heaven and Earth, then hold up flat right hand. (C) Neither is Cheyenne, but both are understood by them. See Promise. With right G make a small cross over the heart. (Pop.)
Make sign for Listen. For emphasis add Yes. Disobey is putting the flat hands over the ears.
See Have to. (C)
Sign Water and Very broad. Some add Salt.
Hold right G hand out straight from right side, palm up; rotate the hand so the index tip describes a small half-circle inward and finally the palm is down. (D) Compare Luck.
Hold the flat hands, palms up, near you, then move forward as though offering something. (Blackfoot.) The Cheyennes sign Want and Give.
Sign Writing House.
Sign Chief; or, if military, sign Chief, Soldier. Or indicate with the right index on the left shoulder or arm the insignia of the particular rank.
See Child.
See Many times.

note.note: Blowing oil off surface in pot.
note.sign: With both L hands form a big level circle; then tilt it low on forward side and blow across it. (Blackfoot, but understood by Cheyennes.)
Hold out the flat left, thumb up; with right thumb on palm and right index on back (others closed); rub back and forth with short, quick jerks. Compare Thick, Thin, Bacon.

Hold right A hand, back to right, about twelve inches in front of right shoulder, about height of breast; move the hand a little upward, to front, downward and back into its first position on small curve, repeating motion. Compare Lame.
Hold up the right index finger as in Man, then drop, crook, and swing it in Old. See Decrepit.
Sign Question, Snows, You; or Question, Counting, Colds, You.
Lay the flat right hand, palm down, on back of the flat horizontal left, palm down. Compare At.
Dip the finger ends of the right compressed hand down against the palm of the flat left hand, bringing it away quickly. For Twice, do it twice, etc. (Sheeaka.) Compare This and Repeat.
Sign One with right G index and push it forward low down. (So, also, Twice is Two pushed forward, etc.)
See Doer.
Sign Potato and Bad, Smell.
See Alone.
The flat hands together, palm to palm, opened out flat as a book. The same as Book with Writing omitted. Compare Day, Book, Shell, and Shut.

Hold out the flat right, fingers doubled on palm, thumb straight up; move it forward level. The thumb represents the tail. This is an Australian sign given by E. C. Stirling. It is offered as a suggestion and as a reminder that the Sign Language is world-wide. The Cheyennes sign Tree, Climb, Hang by tail.

headword.sign: See Either. Sometimes use Different.
See Ready.
See Numbers Ordinal.
See Another.
See Beyond.

With right thumb, index and middle fingers together, others closed, describe a small spiral from near the right ear down. Because the otter skin was the kind used in strips to wrap the plaits of the Indians' hair.
Sign All, My.
See Absent.
Make a semicircle of the left arm out level; drop the compressed right hand without and beyond the semicircle. Compare In, which it resembles, except in the last movement.
See Above.
See Kill.

Hold out flat left hand at arm's length, palm forward, fingers pointing up; hold the right G hand near the breast, palm out, pointing up; move it forward till it strikes the left hand. Use the left G if only one is pursued. Compare Arrive there.
Write on the left palm and swing it from you to me or otherwise, according to the case. (Sheeaka.)
Sign Trade, Time, Money, Give.
Sign Bird and Big-eyes; the latter by putting around each eye a half-circle of thumb and index. (For Horned Owl indicate the horns with G hands.) For Burrowing Owl, sign Owl, Hole, and Dancing.
See Possession.

Hold out left flat hand, back to left (this is the horse); bring right flat hand and place palm against left thumb, fingers pointing to front (this is the right pack); raise the right hand and place palm against upper part of left (this is the left pack); repeat these motions quickly.
See Ache.
Use all the fingers of right hand as a brush painting the left palm. Recent Cheyenne.
Sign Red, then rub the cheeks and front of the face with palm of flat right hand moved in small circles.
Both flat hands, backs up, near breast, shaking.
See Mountain Lion.
With G fingers outline a square, then make as though to write on it with right G.
The index fingers of G hands laid side by side, not touching and not moving. Some make right index point to left and left to right in this. Compare Equal, Race, and Marry.

Hold both L hands, palms up, near the neck, one on each side; sweep them up, over, forward and down, as though removing a halter; at the finish the index fingers are pointing forward and down. Sometimes add Go. See Excuse and Free.
If one-half, indicate it as in the sign for that word; if less, hold the right hand nearer end of index, according to portion desired to be represented. See Half and Some.
See Mate.
See Born.
See Avoid.
Make the sign for Time and jerk the thumb backward over the right shoulder, all fingers closed. (Sheeaka.) Or throw the flat hand back over shoulder. In general, sign Time, Back. See Ago and Back.
See Corral.
See Band.
See Indian Tribes.

House and hold left hand up with thumb, first and second fingers pointing straight down; then make a hoop of right thumb and index and apply it in succession to the three hanging tips. (Pop. and acceptable to Indians.)
See Fins.

headword.note: i.e., “Will you give me?” or “Will you pay?”.
headword.sign: Hold the right hand forward at level of waist, palm up, fingers half closed, rubbing the tip of first finger and tip of thumb together. (Popular and understood by Sheeaka.) The Cheyennes sign Money, Give me.
Clasp the hands in front of body.
Sign Mountain, Part; then hold up high all fingers of right hand in a point, back under.
Sign Plant, then with right index and thumb as in Little of tap five or six times in a row along the side of the left G.
See Fisher.
Sign Red, Money.
Hold up the 5 hands, points up, and add All. Compare Nation and Dance.
Hold up both G hands at various heights, as in Man sign. (Sheeaka.)
Sign Black, then use right O hand as tho it held a sprinkler. A Blackfoot sign understood by Cheyennes.
See If.
See Free.
See If and Consider.
See Spirit.
Use Done both No. 1 and No. 2.
Hold both fists near breast and firmly push them forward once or twice. That is, sign Push repeatedly.
Sign Man.

Hold out the nearly flat left hand at arm's length, face high, palm to you, fingers level, pointing to right; from near it, draw back right fist, palm to left and up, thumb out straight, as though drawing something to the eye; then near the face change the right hand to flat, slightly curved, back forward, fingers pointing to left, and push it forward against palm of left, as in Print.
Indicate the subject, then hold up both L hands to outline bottom and two sides of a square. With imaginary pencil in right draw on this; left remaining as it was. See Portrait and Photograph.

Sign Bread, Round (i.e., with right G, indicate a horizontal circle of proper size), and Sweet. Then sidewise slide the flat right exactly over the flat left, both with palms up. A description, rather than a sign.
See Little of.
See Hog.
Hold out right G breast high, back down, with index curled up, pointing forward; jerk it forward once or twice.
See Gun.

on another (Cry or shed tears for you). Hold G hands, palms downward, index fingers up, in front of and near heart, few inches apart, equally advance and same height; move the hands outward and slightly downward, or toward person. Compare Cry.
Hold G hands well out in front of body, as described above, but with backs out; bring them toward body, slightly raising them.
Hold out flat left, back up; swing compressed right over onto it, then open the right a little. Sometimes omit flat left, or use instead compressed left hand held points up. Compare Bet.
With right G pointing down, indicate a large circle on the ground. (A Pai-ute sign given by Mallery p. 500, also a popular sign.)
With right fingers and thumb, open as though to drop a seed, then closed and moved on to drop another farther, and another, all in the same row. Compare Animal and Jump. See Sow.

Hold up both slightly curved 5 hands, points up and forward, palm to palm, about eight inches apart. Swing them from side to side together, rotating them so the palms are once to front, once to back on each swing. Compare Dance and Children.
Hold out in front of shoulder the slightly curved 5 hand, palm up; rotate slightly by wrist action. See Joke and Laugh.
Push forward with both 5 hands, palms first, three times (i.e., sign Many); then raise the hands very high, palms forward and down. (Sheeaka.) Or sign Heap or Full. Compare Many.
Hold both fists forward as though holding plough, elbows high; and push forward. Also used for cultivation in general.

Sign Vine; that is, hold left forearm upright, as in Tree, and with right G finger trace a climbing Vine about it; then with thumb and first two fingers of right hand, scratch on edge of flat left, held out back up.
See Skunk.
Place the curved right index and thumb, little finger out, against left coat lapel. Compare Medal, Brand, and Name.
Sign Policeman and House.
See Consider.
With right G finger scrape down the left G finger held up, from tip to base, several times. Compare Indian Apache, and Shame.
See Thin.
Sign Hair; then with tips of right 5 hand strike or prick the left palm, held facing the right.
Hold up the flat left hand, back forward, as though it were a Mirror, then sketch on the same with an imaginary pencil, add Face and indicate the person. See Photograph and Picture.
See Comparative.
Curved 5 right hand held as low as possible, back down.
Hold out the left O hand, back to left, and pour into it with the right O hand.
Hold out left hand, palm up; just above it, rub thumb and finger tips of right. Or commonly omit left hand. Compare Dust.
See Can.
Flat hands side by side, palms up; then slowly wide spread on same plane. In conversation, usually but one hand is used. Compare Free and Broad.
See Grouse.
Sign Mound, Hole; then push right G up through hole and add Talk.
See Applause.
Lay the flat hands palm to palm, point them to the sky, then draw down toward self; repeat. (Sheeaka.)
Look up, sign Talk (No. 1.) straight.
Same as Now.
See Beautiful.

Draw the flat hand, palm down, over face to breast; throw back head, look up and add Good. (Blackfoot.) This is their sign for Beautiful with the addition of the head thrown back. Or sign He, Think, He, Big Chief. See Conceit.
Sign Robe and Black.
Push the back of right flat curved hand slowly and firmly against the palm of the left curved ditto, as in Picture, only several times. Compare Quandary, Approach, Photograph etc.
Sign for House, then hold 4 hands up, side by side for prison bars. Add Man and Look through.
Sign Prisoner and House.
Clinch the fists and cross the wrists as though bound, and press down a little.
See Secret.
See Result.
Sign Hill up high, then lay right G against it, pointing up, palm to self, back of right against left hand. See Famous.

Place the forefinger of right G perpendicularly against mouth; bring down fist and, parallel with it, the other fist, thumbs up; strike both down together twice. (Sheeaka.) Also see Word of Honor, or Cross my Heart.
Sign Talk (i.e., Word), Give.
Tap the chest with tips of flat right hand, then raise it, palm forward, and add Talk. (Sheeaka.) Compare Oath.
See Show.
See Offer.
See Defend.
The same as Begin, which see.
See Place.
See Rank.

Hold out the curved left hand nearly at arm's length, back forward; push the ditto right from near the breast right out briskly and hard against the left. Sometimes use Against. Compare Approach, which is similar, but is slow, and right does not touch; also, Print, which pushes and is repeated.
Hold up the left 4 hand, back out; then with the right G turn the little finger down on the palm. Sometimes sign Half, then again half of the tip portion.

headword.note: Two persons springing at each other.
headword.sign: Hold up both G hands and alternately jerk left at right and right at left.
Fire and Wipe out.

headword: I am asking you a question

headword.sign: Sign Question and Work and Way.

Sign Question; next hold the left hand open, curved, palm up, fingers spread; then with right G digit, quickly tap each finger on left in succession, closing it back toward the left palm, beginning with the little finger.

headword.note: As in “What are you doing?” “What is it?”.

note.sign: If seeking a definite answer as to length of time, make signs for Question, How many? and then specify time by sign for hours, days, etc. If asking in general When? sign Question and Time.

If asking for an exact date or point hold up the left G, make a circle around its tip with right G, which always points at it. On reaching the starting point, the right G stops, touches the tip of left G. (Sioux, given by Sheeaka.) This probably represents the shadow going around the tree. See Time.
Strike to left with right G, back up, then over to right a foot away, then back and again; point to the person and sign Come. Usually it needs no Question.

Sign Question; then with forefinger sweep the horizon in a succession of bounds, a slight pause at the bottom of each, the head following the finger. (Sioux and Arapahoe.) The actual line of the finger is illustrated in the lower plan, the hand being gracefully rotated on the wrist in doing it. Or sign Question and Somewhere.
Sign Question and Look.
Extend the open hands, palm up, from the sides out low to the front, and swing them from side to side with a look of inquiry on the face. (Pop.)

note.note: When the objects are in sight.
note.sign: Sign Question and point with right G in three or four directions, downward or toward the objects in question.

note.note: When the objects are not in sight.
note.sign: Sign Question; then hold left hand in front of you, with palm toward you, fingers to right and held apart; place the end of the right forefinger on that of the left forefinger and then draw it down across the other fingers.
Sign Question and Go, in two or three directions.
Sign Question and Man.
Sign Question, but do it very slowly. (C)
Sign Question and Want.
See Fast and Hurry.
The palm of the flat hand held toward the person and gently depressed once or twice. See Easy.
See Silence.
See Give up; also Finish.

Move the M hand straight to the front, back up and undulating on the wrist, to imitate the rabbit hopping forward; then make V right hand and turn it to look back. (Scott.) The Cheyennes omit the second part of this.
Draw the V hand horizontally across the face and nose. If necessary, also indicate Size and striped tail.
Move the index fingers forward and up, side by side, as in Equal; but keep them moving a long way forward and upward. Compare Parallel, Marry.
Touch Coat, add Old; then hold left 5 slightly curved, back up and use ditto right as though to comb out the fingers of left, once or twice.
Sign Fire twice upward for puffs, then add Fast. Compare Motor car.
Indicate Train as above; then push the right G finger quickly along the back of the left V hand and on beyond. (Sheeaka.)
Sign Road; then with the two G hands, backs up, indicate rails as in Parallel.
Sign Hard (i.e., metal); then hold out two G fingers, backs up, six inches apart, and push both together far forward and a little up.
Make the signs of Railway and House; adding, if necessary, Alight and Aboard.
Hold A hands, backs up, opposite forehead, near each other; lower them slightly, mostly by wrist action; at the same time open and separate fingers and thumb so they point downward; repeat.
Sign Rain then indicate the arch with a slow sweep of the flat right hand, back up, high above head.
Sign Chief; then lay upright right G, palm forward, against back of left ditto, as in Rising man, sliding the right up and down to various heights.
Sign Chief, Big; Chief, Little; Question. Sometimes omit Chief, Little.
See Fast.
Sign River, Rock; and pass the right 5 hand, back up, points first, swiftly forward and down, in an up and down waved course.
Hold left hand on eyes and point right G index forward, moving it to front.
Sign Snake, then hold right G finger, pointing up, near shoulder, and shake it.
Arrive there.

Extend the open hands, palm to palm, a few inches apart, pointing outward and parallel to each other, over toward the left side; lift them both together from the wrists, move toward the right a little and let them come down again; repeat the motion until by stages the hands have been moved over to the right side. (D)
Sign All, Good; or use Arranged, or combine them into Arranged, All, Good. See Arranged.
See Bring back.
Hold out hollow right hand, palm up, half open; draw it back, slightly closing fingers. Compare Give to me.

Hold right G hand, back up, in front of breast, pointing to left and front; raise the hand with a graceful sweep, at same time turn it back to front and index pointing upward. The actual course of the index tip, if seen from above, is as in the dotted line under the hand.
See Play.
See Decrease.
See Idea and Consider.
See Won't.
Sign Medicine and Way.
See Sit.
Sign Heart, Know. Or, in popular code, touch the forehead with right G, raise the brows and nod.
Hold right G index upright and grasp it firmly with left hand, face high.
As above, but let the right G index slip down and out. See Forget.
See Memories.

Place the finger tips of the compressed right hand on the left palm, as the latter is held in front of the body, back down, and strike once or twice. (Sheeaka. Probably borrowed from Deaf.) Compare Once, Twice, Often, and More.
Hold up right G, chin high, back up, pointing to left and forward; lash down with it like a whip twice or more as best fits in. Sometimes sign Come back. Compare All the time.
See Answer.
See Beg.
See Alike.
See Answer.
Sign I (or whoever it is), Do, That Or My (or His) Way. (Seger.) Sign Carry and That (Suggested.)
Sign for House and Eat.
Sign Hold and Keep quiet. Sometimes use Do not.
Sign After, Work, See.
Sign Charge, then reverse and withdraw the hands.
Sign Going, then turn the G hand palm toward you and draw it back with similar action. (Understood; not established.)
Bow the head, resting the mouth on the A fist. Compare Memories.
See Blackguarding.
See Recover.
Sign for drawing from belt behind and present the same, using right G hand, back to right. Add Fire-off, if need be. See Gun.
Sign Possesses, Heap, Money.
Hold the hands as in Horse, and then move the hands to the front on short vertical curves.
Hold the A hands touching, thumbs toward face and upright; draw them apart a foot. Compare Soldiers and Hill.

Sign Hill with right, then hold 5 out at arm's length, face high, flat, and bent, so the fingers point to the left; swing it slowly horizontally across to the right. This last seems to mean “lying across the horizon” and appears in several combinations. See Mirage.
See Gun.
See Good.
See Creek.
Hold up left 5 hand; then with right index and thumb make as though slipping a ring on the ring finger.

note.note: Man rising to stand on a hill.
note.sign: Hold up left as in Hill; lay right G behind or beside it, against the thumb, palm forward, pointing up; push right up until the base of the index is sitting on top of the left; that is, becomes Prominent. See Prominent and Famous.
Sign Water then with tremulous movement draw flat right 4 hand, palm down, from opposite left breast to opposite right; fingers always level and pointing to left. Compare Creek and Rill.

note.note: i.e., Highroad; especially between high banks, hills, or fences.
note.sign: Holding the open hands, palm to palm and pointing forward, carry them forward, as if they represented the sides of a road; then add Going by pushing the flat right hand forward in line between, palm to left, fingers level. (Sheeaka.) Or sign Way and Wagon.
Sign Coat; but instead of ending at waist, sweep the hands as low as possible.
Sign Hard and sometimes indicate shape. For Stone add Lump. Compare Metal.

First sign Tree or Grass, as may be; then point down, place the 5 hands together at the wrists, backs up and level; pointing left to front and left, right to front and right; then move them out and apart.

note.note: Trailing after the horse and twisted.
note.sign: Sign After; then, as right is drawn to rear, make tip of index describe a spiral curve. Commonly omit the left hand.

Hold the fingers of the left hand straight, little separated, arranged in a circle, back to left and front; in front of body, index finger horizontal and pointing to right and front; with right hand make as though picking berries from the left finger tips. (C)
Indicate smell, etc.
Put middle finger of right hand to tongue, then rub left palm and wipe the palm with under side of right forearm. (Sheeaka. A white man's sign now understood by Indians.)

With flat 5 right hand, palm down, shoulder high, swing out level from throat to right, working all the fingers as in playing piano. (A Paiute sign, given me by Mary Austin.) A combination of Wind and Fly.
Swing the fists at each side as in running.
See Quandary.
Hold out flat left hand, palm down; push right G hand under it quickly and sinuously. Sometimes preface it by laying one hand over the eyes. Compare Jealous.
See River.
See Medicine.
See Sorrow.
Hold out both S hands, palms up, side by side (sometimes inches apart), about height of shoulders, forearms vertical, wrists bent so backs of hands are nearly down.
See Recover or Alive.
With all finger tips together pointing up, swing the hand to various places in front of right shoulder. (C) Compare Peak and Canoe.
Sign White, Good smell, Grass.
Sign Powder, then the act of sprinkling with finger and thumb. (Blackfoot.) Touch the tongue cautiously with the right G. Compare Sugar and Pepper.
See Equal.
See Bar up.
See Contented.
See Recover, also Free.
See But.
This word universal in the west is the same sign as Know.
With lower edge of right, thumb up, saw across the upper edge of left wrist held out horizontally.
See Call.
Point to scalp, make as though pulling it forward and sign Cutting under. The last two gestures being done out in front of the body.
See Go and Scatter.

Hold the closed hands, backs up, near each other and close to breast; move right hand well to front and right, left well to front and left; that is, widely separate the hands, swinging each in a half-circle out, so the palms are a little outward, at the same time extending and separating fingers and thumbs. (C) Compare Bad and Scorn; in these the hands are not moved apart.
Swing closed right to left, there opening to 5 so palm is to left and forward; then same movement with hand opened to right, each time in a graceful sweep. The Cheyennes say the above is Throw away.
Sign House and Writing; usually preceded by Children.
Sign Book and Chief.
See Abuse; also Fault-finding.
Turn the head away and with one hand throw an imaginary handful of sand toward the feet of the person. This is the same as Bad, except for the turn of the head.
Same as Wolf, but hold the hand near the right ear. Sometimes use Advance Guard, which see. The Cheyennes sometimes sign it as Wolf, Soldier.
Sign Wolf and Look.

headword.sign: Sign Boy Scout; then lay the 4 left hand on left side of head to indicate plume. (Suggested.)
Make signs Boy Scout and Small. (Suggested.)
Right fist closed, thumb held up straight and free, then used as a trowel on an imaginary wall. (Pop.)
With a hand grasping each lapel, spread open the coat. (Pop.)
The four seasons are Little Grass (Spring); High Grass (Summer); Leaf Fall (Autumn); and Cold or Snow (Winter). Each is given in alphabetic place.
Left hand flat, horizontal, near left cheek; with right, sign Talk under it. Usually sign Talk and Hide.
See Hunt.

The fingers of V hand pointed forward (as in Look) then advanced a little in the line of sight; sometimes for extra point, it is changed into G hand and pushed forward. Also compare Hunt and Lie, Look and Find. The difference between Look and See is not observed by most Indians; but it is well to maintain it.
Point at one's own chin with the right V hand and touch breast.
See Appear.
Move the open hands forward; grasp and draw back as though seizing some object.
See Choose.
See Trade. On the Stock Exchange, the clenched fist thrown forward and down means Sell. Probably in imitation of the auctioneer's hammer. See Kill.

On middle of side of left G held out, tap two or three times with middle side of right G. This is also used for Buy, which see for illustration. It is supposed to have had origin in an old gambling game. See Trade.
Hold the right A near the breast; swing it out, up and down a foot; then swing the right G higher and farther. (Sheeaka.)
Lay the G fingers side by side, backs up; spring them apart, widest at tips, moving them forward and out.
Extend the fingers of the right A hand, one at a time, beginning with the index. (Sheeaka.)

Hold flat left hand index edge up, thumb level with index; move right G hand index with extended thumb, across left index once or twice as in sewing; each time nearer the body and each time turning the right index nail down, as it is moved forward. Compare Awl.
Sign Sun, Not.
Indicate the person; then sign Going, There by me, Same. There by me is indicated by pointing to the ground on the left side with right G.
See Will.
See Medicine-man.

(The finger of scorn made sharper.) Point left index at person, all others closed; and with right index similarly held, rub it on back of left index from middle to tip and beyond. (Pop.) See Ashamed. In France the idea is conveyed by the Horns. See Evil Eye.
Hold out flat right hand, palm up; touch little finger edge lightly with ball of left thumb and add Good. For Sharp points see Porcupine.
Use the flat right hand as a razor, palm to right, points up, little finger next right cheek as the edge; move it toward the right ear.
Sign Blanket and Fringe. For Fringe, hold out the left 5 hand points forward, level; then push the similar right over it forward several times.
Sign Female and point with G finger at the person.
With compressed hands above each eye, pointed backward, trace the sweep of horns, ending below ears, with points turned forward.
Sign Bighorn and White Man.
Hold the curved hands side by side, close and open them on the under side, as though hinged on top. Compare Boat, Book, Bowl, and Open.
With both L hands a little apart, index fingers pointed down, make a large incomplete circle to left of left breast. See Protect.
Hold out the curved right 5 hand, palm down; lower it slightly, shaking it quickly sidewise. Compare Snow, Glitter, and Easy.
Raise and spread thumb and two first fingers to be masts, others closed; then push the hand forward slowly in a rising and falling line. (Deaf sign for Sailing Ship. For Indian sign see Boat, Big.)
Sign Moccasin, White Man.
See Fire.
Sign Bow, then snap the index fingers out straight. To add Hit with an arrow, hold up the flat left and thrust the right G through it.
See Store.

For things which grow, hold the flat hand back forward, fingers pointing up at desired height. For things which do not grow, use the flat hand, palm down; or else, both flat hands side by side, palm to palm. The same as Low.
See Gun.
See Yell.
Raise flat left hand, palm forward. Lay index of right G on it and turn the two about, pushing them forward as though to show something. (Sheeaka. Borrowed from the Deaf.)
Hold out the flat left, palm up, pointing forward and down. Point to the person in question with right G, then at left palm with right V.
Hold flat hands out near breast; move the hands quickly outward and back several times. Compare Lungs.
See Parallel.
Touch the back of left hand with tip of right, the back of right with tip of left and add Talk. (C)
Lay the extended index, pointing upward, over the mouth. In the more vigorous form of Shut up, lay the flat hand on the mouth (recent).
See Foolish.
Sign Money and White.
Same as Bad. See Evil.
See After.

Hold right V hand, back to right, in front of face; finger tips a little higher than and close to mouth, pointing nearly up. Move the hand briskly so finger tips describe a small horizontal circle. Compare Lie and Abuse.
See Indian.
Sign Woman and Brother.
Sign Brother-in-law and Woman.
Hold the right A hand in front of and a little lower than right shoulder, back to right; move the hand emphatically downward a few inches. Compare Wait and Aboard.
See Hide.
See Lean.
Indicate Size and tail up; that is, curving G up, palm forward; move in gentle jerks forward, and then add Smell, Bad. Compare Weasel.
See Heavens.
Both G hands, backs down, 4 inches apart; index fingers curved and pushed forward. The idea is helped by Snow on Ground.

Hold both flat hands, backs up, in front of breast, same level; swing both over to the left in an up and down curve, in which the right (only) turns palm up; the left continues back up; then lower the head a little to right. Some finish with both palms up.
Rub the eyes with the fists. (Pop.) Or yawn and lay the head on one side, closing the eyes. (Sheeaka.) Both of these are understood by the Cheyennes, but they use Want, Sleep.

Hold out the flat left hand, palm to right; ditto right hand opposite, palm to left; in slow jerks, move the left hand forward; at the same time, jerk the right, making the latter fall behind. Compare Fast.

Compress both hands so the fingers are straight, but at an angle with the back of the hand; hold them about eight inches apart, backs out, pointed up and forward, right higher; move them together till the right is over the left. Sometimes the Cheyennes made this sign with the closed fists to mean Few. Compare Little.
See Decrease.
See Cunning.
Hold V hand, back up, fingers pointing to chin; swing fingers up so the nose passes between them. Compare Blood and Brother. Place the palm close before the tip of the nose. (Pop.)
Sign Smell and hold the nostrils, or sign Bad. (Sheeaka.)
Sign Smell and Good.
For distant smoke, like a signal-fire smoke, make sign for Fire and continue raising hand in a spiral till higher than head.
Hold up the left A, thumb up, tap on top two or three times with flat right, then add Pipe, jerking it forward two or three times.
Put index and thumb to mouth as though holding a cigarette. (Blackfoot.)
Rub the back of the flat left hand, held palm down, with whole palm of the flat right, back and forth, in long strokes. Sometimes use Prairie. Compare Indian and At.
See Prairie.
Hold the right H (or sometimes G) hand, back to right, waist high, fingers pointing to front; move it several inches to front in a sinuous line from side to side. Compare Fish, Creek, and Rope.
See Run away.

Raise and spread both hands a foot apart, backs up, fingers curved; then softly lower them in slow, short zigzags. For Rain, the lines down are straight. Years are commonly called Snows or Winters. Compare Shimmer.
Swing the right G index from 45 degree angle down to level; then add Straight, swinging index up slightly at finish. Sometimes sign Yes. Compare Idea.
Strike down a foot with the right G in front of breast, then strike it on the left flat hand held opposite left breast. (Sheeaka.)
Rub the hands together as in washing them.
Form a horizontal half-circle of left index and thumb; drop the right fist into it; reverse the hands and repeat.
See Earth.
Hold the flattened fists out in front, side by side, backs up so basal joints of the fingers are nearly level and the middle joints form a row; then swing them apart.
Point with index finger downward and at some near spot on the ground, then change to another farther off, then to another. (Scott.)

Hold out level flat left, back forward and out; lay flat right thumb up on left index near the tip; then jerk it toward tip and beyond. This is much like Halve; but the right is casually brushed along the edge of the left and at no time precisely placed.
Sign Time, moving the hands apart in short jerks. (Seger.) Compare By and by.
Hold right G up near right shoulder; swing it in a curve up and down to opposite left shoulder and back. Also used in the question Where?
Sign Born and Male.
Sign for Sing and Write.
Sign Time, then stop with the fingers an inch apart. Compare Sometimes, Time, By and by, Close.
Lay the right compressed hand on the heart, pointing down; then throw it forward and down, ending the sign with the flat hand very low, palm up, near the ground.
Rub the fist on heart in circle two or three times. (Blackfoot.)
See Spirit.
See Bitter.
See Sew.
See Scatter.
See Fire.
See Talk.
Make as though thrusting a spear with both hands.

That is, a long talk, as at council, by oneself. (Handing out words.) Hold the flat right hand, back down, pointing to left, at lower lip and swing it forward several times. Compare Discussion and Talk.
Speech by another addressed to oneself is the same as the preceding, but swing the right hand in toward the chin instead of out. Compare Drink, Water, Discussion, and Talk.
Make writing in the air, then sign Talk.
Hold the palm of the right hand about one and a half inches from a flat surface; spread the fingers and thumb and work them like legs; the hand represents the body. (Scott.) Compare Bunch and Herd.
There is no established sign; but Sheeaka understood this combination: Water and Strong. A Cheyenne gave it Lantern, Different, Strong, Pour.
See Glow.
Use the cupped right hand as a spoon.

Hold out the level left arm and with right curved 5 hand finger tips tap the left arm at various places on the inside, from the wrist up, as though flecking it with the tip of a brush, at both up and down strokes. Compare Striped.

Make the sign for Water, then a large horizontal circle with L hands (as in Hole); then push the right compressed hand up through the left L hand and, as it comes up, extend the fingers with a slight snap to represent the bubbling water.
Sign Grass and Short. Add Time if there is doubt.
See Few.
Hold both compressed hands near the mouth and give a quick motion of the lips. (Ruggles.) Or sign Tree with left hand, then with compressed right hand as in Animal indicate running up and around.
Sign Squirrel, Fly; and then with right hand flat, palm down, simulate flight down and up in a long sweep. This is merely a description, not an established sign.
Make right V hand stand on left palm, the fingers representing legs. For an animal, use all four fingers of right. See Alight.
Hold G hand, back forward, erect over shoulder. Compare Tall and Up.

Make the sign for Night, then cross the right G with left G near the tips and hold up high. Some flirt the index tip from behind the curved thumb in different directions up high, to mean twinkling. This is nearly like Talk up high.
Sign Stop, Wait, and Sit down.
Hold out flat left hand pointing out, back up; reach right G hand under wrist, then draw it back with a sweep, curving it into a hook at the same time.
Sign Boat, Big, Fire, holding the hand above the forehead for the last.
See Mean.
Sign Bad Smell.
Hold left as in C, back out, and make as though stirring its contents with a ladle held in right. Compare Tea.
Sign Rock and Lump.
See Halt.
See Period.
Sign House and Trade.
Sign Strong then Wind, shaking the fingers when opened and sometimes adding the sound of blowing.
See History.
See Honest; also True.
Sign, My, People, Not. Or Man, Different.
See River.
Hold out flat left, palm up; strike it with the edge of flat right hand. Compare Chop and Kill.
See Count Coup.
Hold out flat left with forearm level and draw the right flat palm across it at different points on the upper side. Compare Spotted.

With left fist, back out, grasp an imaginary stick; then also grasp it four inches higher with right fist, back in. Give a strong outward twist to the right, finishing with the right fist below the left and back downward. The left is not moved. This means physically strong in most cases, and few Indians distinguish this from Very much; which see. Compare Little.

Holding out the flat left, palm toward you, with all the right fingers and thumb make as though seizing something on the left palm; draw the right to you and down. (Sheeaka. Borrowed from the Deaf.) Compare Place.
Sign Push, Work, and Finish. Compare Fail.
See Sick.
Rub the tongue with tips of extended index and second finger of right hand, then add Good. Compare Salt and Bitter.
Sign Heart, Angry, Hide. (Blackfoot.) See Gloomy.
Sign High, Grass. Or sometimes sign Hot only.
Form a circle with index and thumb of right hand; hold hand toward east and swing it in a great up-curve toward the west. In conversation, the circle is often incomplete.
Sign Day and Medicine.
is Day after Medicine Day.
is Two Days after Medicine Day.
is Three Days after Medicine Day.
is Four Days after Medicine Day.
is Two Days before Medicine Day.
is Little Medicine Day.
Make a ring of the right index and thumb, others closed, level, at full length toward the east; then raise it, chiefly by wrist action, so the ring is nearly at an angle of 45 degrees.
The reverse of Sunrise; that is, hold the ring to the west and swing it down from 45 degrees to level or lower.
Hold both G fingers side by side, upright, one of them higher to represent the person or thing. When it is One above many use the left “5” hand instead of left G. See Rising Man, also Chief.
See Comparative; also Very much.
Sign Night and Eat.

Hold flat hand on the mouth. This can be made stronger by using both hands. Sometimes also for emphasis precede this with a slap down of the flat right on the flat left, palm to palm. See Astonishment.

note.sign: Hold out both L hands at arm's length, then swing them together to form a level circle. See Enclosure.
See Trade.
See Oath.
Draw the hooked right index across the brow as though wiping off sweat. See Hot.
Sign Medicine and Wickey up. (C) Or with 5 hands indicate the shape beginning at top, then sign Open, Enter, Sweat.
See Sugar.
Touch G to lips and add Heart. (Modern, but now in general use among Cheyenne boys.)
Thrust the right L hand forward, level, back up and to right, turning slowly by wrist action so the thumb rises two or three inches up and down on the axis of the index. Compare Courting and Glitter.
See Fast.

note.note: Probably to suggest a fish tail in action.
note.sign: That is, “Will you come in swimming?” Hold right hand as high as the face, back forward, all fingers closed except index and middle, these are spread like V and pointed to left. Move the hand a little to right (Pop.)
Sign Water, then strike out with hands as in swimming.
See Dive.
Swing flat hands, palms down, as in Broad turn sharply and draw both toward you; with a V hand on each side, strike down for legs, then sign On and Eat. A description, not an established sign.
Right G hand, back up, at left side, pointing back and down.
Reach out the G hand, hook the index and draw it toward you, in and upward, as though pulling a string up and back. Compare Steal.
The same, but point index toward body, hook it and draw away.
See Subtract.

Hold right hand under mouth, index and thumb tips together, pointing forward, and move slightly forward, snapping the index from behind the thumb two or three times. Compare Called, Telltale, Speech, Bark.
Make the same gesture as above, but point and draw the hand toward the cheek.
Move the flat right straight up to arm's length, back out.
Revolve the 5 hands, in and out, one about the other. Compare Play.
Join the first finger and thumb, rest their points on the lower lip and work the lips. Compare Salt, Sugar, Sour, and Bitter.
Sign Taste and Bad. (Seger.)
See Telltale.
Tap the place with all five finger tips in a point.
Trace the rim of the left O hand with the thumb and finger tip of the right O hand, other fingers extended. (Sheeaka.) Compare Stir. Or sign Leaf, Drink.
Sign Writing and Chief. See also Guide.
Sign Horse, Two; with L hand indicate Halter and add Coat for Harness.
Cross the tips of the G fingers held high. In Tent they are not crossed.
On forefinger of left G hand, palm up, tap with crooked forefinger of right hand, as though telegraphing; then shoot it along left forefinger and on in line. (Crow sign, La Forge.)
Sign Wire, then tap on it two or three times with right G and add Talk, shooting it far ahead.
Sign Wire; raise O hand to the ear like the receiver, then add Talk.
See Explain and Speech.
See Talk and Speech.
Make a bill with forefinger and thumb; hold it at the mouth, pointing forward; open and shut it, but do not advance it. Note, it does not get anywhere; Talk does.
Make signs for Scout and Little. (Scott.)

headword.sign: Like Teepee, but do not cross the fingers. Sometimes add White Man.
See As.

Raise the open right hand within a foot of the face, back down and to right; then carry it outward and downward toward person, bowing at same time (Pop). For this the Cheyennes use one hand as in Gratitude, which see. Sign Give, Good. (Blackfoot.)
Point with right G at the person or thing. Compare This, There, and Yonder.
Hold out right G, back up, at arm's length, face high, pointing forward; strike it down three or four times, chiefly by finger action. Compare Then and Here.
Sign House, Look, Big, Many. (Sheeaka.)
See Possession.
Swing right G, point first, forward and down in an 18-inch curve. Compare Yonder and That place.
Simply point with middle finger, others closed, hand held breast high. Compare Challenge.

Hold out flat left, back to left, place right underneath palm up; clasp left with it, rub right fingers and thumb tips back and forth on the middle of the left hand in long, slow rubs. Compare Meat, Thin, Oil, and Bacon.
Sign for the person and add Steal. (C)

Like Thick, but rub lower edge and little finger of left with tips of right thumb and index finger united, others closed. Usually the little finger side is the Edge. Compare Bacon, in which all of the fingers are used; also, Oil, Thick, and Meat.
Bring both 5 hands, backs forward, in front of breast and touching it. Move each to its side, curving the fingers more.
The 5 hands similarly pointing forward, backs up, waist high, one at each side of the body; swing once or twice in small circles nearly vertical, but a little forward in the upper part.
Lay right G on the heart, back up, and swing it outward ten inches and a little up.
See Consider.
Sign Want and Drink.
Hold flat left palm up near body and thrust right G down to it. For These repeat it several times.
See Idea.
Sign Hundred and then Ten times. See Numbers.
Rub thumb and index together, as though twisting a thread, and sign Sew. Compare Powder.
Shake clenched fist toward the person.
Push the right flat hand edgewise outward between the middle and third fingers of the left, which are held pointing upward. Compare Between.
Clap the hands loudly in front of face and add a rapid zigzag with the right G finger for Lightning.
Sign You. Possess.
Hold out H left, back up; lay the right G across it at the middle knuckles; add Writing and indicate Railway, Theatre, Pawn, etc., as needed.
See To.

Join the index finger and thumb of each hand at tips, other fingers closed; hold back of right hand to right, left to left, thumb tips touching; draw the hands apart, slowly and level. A little apart means Little time; a long way is Long time, etc. Sign After, Little time, for Bye and bye. So leaving the hands touching means Now; and Time, Long, Behind means Long time ago. A much-used sign. See Ago and Past.

Hold out the left G level, pointing forward, breast high; lay the right G on the back of the left, draw it back toward the wrist an inch. For Time ahead, sign Time and Ahead, that is, hold up left G and swing right G parallel and far ahead in the same line. Or sometimes for Time ahead or Future give the Time sign first given, but draw the right forefinger and thumb in an up and over curve far ahead, instead of to the right.
Hold up left G, point right G at it and swing it around, finally touching it at top. (Sioux, Sheeaka.) This is used for exact point or date.
Indicate in the sky the position of the sun at that hour.
Hold up the left hand with back toward you, index and thumb joining in a complete vertical circle; with right G on this as a pointer, move an inch and add One.

Hold the flat left hand pointed forward, thumb up; lay the right G on it like a pointer and move it the least bit. (Sheeaka.) The Cheyennes sign Hour and Small. In giving Time it is safest to do it in railway style; that is, 45 minutes after 4 would be 4 hours and 45 minutes; not a quarter before 5.
Make the signs Minute and Very small. (Sheeaka.)

headword.note: The opening up, as contrasted with Night, the closing over.
headword.sign: Hold out the level flat hands in the same horizontal plane, backs up, pointing to front, a few inches apart. Swing them upward apart to right and left, and then downward on a curve, turning the palms up; ending when the hands are about opposite shoulders and a little higher than at the start.
Sign Suns, Seven (Sheeaka) or One Medicine Day. Compare Sunday.
Crescent or Horns in the sky. See illustration in M.
Spring, Short grass; Summer, High grass; Autumn, Falling leaf; Winter, Cold or Snow.

Sign One Cold (Cheyenne). Or better perhaps, though not general, sign Snow, then swing the right G in a circle to left, down and up on right, then again make Snow, meaning from snow around to snow. (Sheeaka.) Sign One Snow (Blackfoot).
Hold out G hands, backs up, six inches apart, drop them and draw them to you a little. Sometimes used for Quit. See Lazy. Compare Afraid.
Hold the left G a little forward, palm to you; swing the right G upward till the forefingers meet at tip. (Sheeaka; probably borrowed from the Deaf.) Compare Meet.
Hold flat left hand, back down, in front of body; grind on it the heel of closed right in small circles.
Sign Day and Now.

Press the palms of the flat hands together two or three times, swinging them apart and together once or twice, so the tips describe six-inch vertical circles going down on the outside and up on the inside of each. Note this also means Gather. See also With, Meet, and Heap.
Hold the flat right hand in the hollow of the horizontal left arm (C). Compare Baby. Or sign Axe and Smoke.
Sign for Another and Sunrise. Compare Yesterday.
Sign Enough, but raise both hands at arm's length above the head. (Scott.) Sometimes use Heap. See also Excessive, Ahead, and Over.
Sign Equal, With, or And.
See Feel.
Sign House; then, keeping the flat hands at same angle, swing them wide apart, keeping left near body, right far away. Add White Man if needed.
Hold G hands pointing up, one at each shoulder; move them together in a down curve till wrists are crossed. See Avoid.
See Way.
See Railroad.
Make a large level circle of forefingers and thumbs for the trap; then snap these up together, index alongside index, thumb alongside thumb to indicate the closing.
Hold out the 5 hands, palm to palm, but left a foot advanced and six inches higher, both of them vibrated up and down. This means Keep on Going. See Work, Go, and Walk.
Sign Shake Hands and Write; that is, write with right index on flat left palm.

Hold right forearm upright in front of shoulder, fingers straight, spread upward. For plural use both hands. For Forest, hold left outside and touching right; draw right near body and push left far away. See Forest.
See Bunch.
Wave one hand in circle above the head as swinging a flag. At a distance, wave a hat, coat, or blanket.
See Bunch.
Indicate the kind of animal, then with S hands, backs up, indicate movement of feet as in trotting. (C)
See Consider, Doubt, Sorry, and Sorrow.
The G forefinger pointing straight forward under the chin, then moved forward with an upward curve. Compare Honest.
Sign Work and Begin.
Sign Bird; then indicate Beard with compressed right hand under the chin, pointed down and shaken. (C)
Sign Bird; then hold right G at forehead, back up, curved, pointed down, and drop it past the nose down below chin.
See Sweat Lodge.
This dates from the Roman arena. Right arm at full length, fingers closed, thumb extended and pointing downward; meaning “Kill him” or “it.” See Knife.
See Grow.
See Free and Pardon.
Hold right hand low, back up, flat, but fingers bent back on palm; push it forward, giving it by wrist action a serpentine course. See Fog.
See Once.
Sign Star and Talk.
Lay V fingers, palm in, on abdomen and add Born.
Sign Write; then with both hands strike here and there at keys.
Sign Face; i.e., swing the right 5 hand in a circle near the face, and add Bad.
See Can't.
Sign Perhaps, Know, and Not.
Sign Father (or Mother) and Brother.
Bow the head forward, resting the right G on the lips. See also Thinking and If.
See Below.
See Know; also, Keep.
See Excessive.
Sign Alliance or With.

There is no well-established sign, but the Indian of a hundred years ago referred to the United States as the “Thirteen Fires,” i.e., Council Fires. So that Thirteen and Fires would answer. The Blackfeet indicate the Boundary Line running east and west, then all south of it.
Country and Cotton. The latter by pointing to something by cotton.
Country, Trees, and Sharp, as in Porcupine.
Country and Bear Black, to distinguish from Russia.
Country and Gold; for gold pinch the lobe of the ear, or point to any gold or yellow thing in sight; if it is near, take an imaginary pinch of it, to exclude the object that is made of it.
Country and 100 Years or Snows.
Country, Fruit, and Hard.
Country and holding G hand, palm up, wag the index to express the tail of the Flickertail gopher.
Country and Small Wolf.
Country and, for Diamond, place the right thumb and index on an imaginary stone on ring finger of left; then add Twinkle.
Country and Water. The half into the left C hand, held level and facing the right, lay the right N fingers, backs up.
Country, Corn, and Grind, as in Coffee. The Crackers were so called because of their cracked corn diet.
Country and Snake.
Country and Prairie.
Country and Who is here?
Country, Hawk, and Eye.
Country, Flower, and Sun.
Country, Grass, and Blue.
Country, Bird, Long bill; and with index show outline of the pouch.
Country and Tree.
Country and Turtle.
Country and Bay.
Country and Wolverine or Bushy-tailed Bear. Indicate Bear, then Tail and Bushy.
Country and Small Striped Animal. With compressed right hand, back up, indicate a small animal; then draw the fingers of left 4 hand along it for stripes.
Country, Bay, and Trees.
Country and Flag. Or else, “Show me State,” thus, with flat right hand shade right eye, knit brows, look here and there.
